abstract: GPU kernel optimization is fundamental to modern deep learning but remains a highly specialized task requiring deep hardware expertise. Despite strong performance in general programming, large language models (LLMs) remain uncompetitive with compiler-based systems such as this http URL for CUDA kernel generation. Existing CUDA code generation approaches either rely on training-free refinement or fine-tune models within fixed multi-turn execution-feedback loops, but both paradigms fail to fundamentally improve the model's intrinsic CUDA optimization ability, resulting in limited performance gains. We present CUDA Agent, a large-scale agentic reinforcement learning system that develops CUDA kernel expertise through three components: a scalable data synthesis pipeline, a skill-augmented CUDA development environment with automated verification and profiling to provide reliable reward signals, and reinforcement learning algorithmic techniques enabling stable training. CUDA Agent achieves state-of-the-art results on KernelBench, delivering 100\%, 100\%, and 92\% faster rate over this http URL on KernelBench Level-1, Level-2, and Level-3 splits, outperforming the strongest proprietary models such as Claude Opus 4.5 and Gemini 3 Pro by about 40\% on the hardest Level-3 setting.

organization_info.name: Institute for AI Industry Research (AIR), Tsinghua University
SIA-Lab of Tsinghua AIR and ByteDance Seed

keyInsights: Large language models can be effectively transformed into autonomous, performance-aware system optimizers for complex hardware domains through agentic reinforcement learning.
Scalable and diverse data synthesis is crucial for overcoming data scarcity in specialized code generation tasks and for enabling large-scale, generalizable RL training.

Robust agent environments, coupled with milestone-based reward signals and multi-stage RL warm-up, are essential for stabilizing training and enabling LLMs to learn complex, multi-turn optimization strategies.
originalProblem: GPU kernel optimization requires highly specialized expertise that is scarce and difficult to scale, posing a bottleneck for modern deep learning infrastructure.
Existing LLM-based approaches for CUDA kernel generation have not been competitive with traditional compiler-based systems like `torch.compile`.
Prior fine-tuning methods for LLMs in code generation suffered from data scarcity, limited training scale, and restrictive, fixed optimization loops.
results: CUDA Agent achieved state-of-the-art performance on KernelBench, demonstrating an overall 98.8% Pass Rate, 98.4% Faster Rate vs. Eager, and 96.8% Faster Rate vs. Compile, with geometric mean speed-ups of 2.60× and 2.11× respectively.

The system significantly outperformed leading proprietary models (e.g., Claude Opus 4.5, Gemini 3 Pro) on complex tasks, achieving a 94.0% Faster Rate vs. Compile and 1.52× speed-up on Level-3 (neural network blocks), roughly 40% higher faster rate than competitors.

Ablation studies confirmed that the skill-integrated agent loop, robust discrete reward design, and multi-stage training (Rejection Fine-Tuning and Value Pretraining) were indispensable for achieving high correctness, optimization quality, and stable RL training.
solution: A scalable, three-stage data synthesis pipeline was developed to generate `CUDA-Agent-Ops-6K`, a large and diverse dataset of PyTorch operator tasks, suitable for large-scale reinforcement learning.

A skill-integrated agent loop enables the LLM to autonomously interact with a development environment, utilizing shell utilities and CUDA-specific instructions (via `SKILL.md`) to iteratively write, debug, and optimize kernels.

Multi-stage warm-up strategies, including Rejection Fine-Tuning for the actor and Value Pretraining for the critic, were implemented to stabilize Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and enable long, consistent RL training.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from ByteDance Seed and Tsinghua University developed CUDA Agent, a large-scale agentic reinforcement learning system that autonomously generates high-performance CUDA kernels. This system achieves superior optimization capabilities on the KernelBench benchmark, yielding substantial speedups over traditional compilers and other advanced language models.

title: CUDA Agent: Large-Scale Agentic RL for High-Performance CUDA Kernel Generation

abstract:
In this white paper, we present AlphaEvolve, an evolutionary coding agent
that substantially enhances capabilities of state-of-the-art LLMs on highly
challenging tasks such as tackling open scientific problems or optimizing
critical pieces of computational infrastructure. AlphaEvolve orchestrates an
autonomous pipeline of LLMs, whose task is to improve an algorithm by making
direct changes to the code. Using an evolutionary approach, continuously
receiving feedback from one or more evaluators, AlphaEvolve iteratively
improves the algorithm, potentially leading to new scientific and practical
discoveries. We demonstrate the broad applicability of this approach by
applying it to a number of important computational problems. When applied to
optimizing critical components of large-scale computational stacks at Google,
AlphaEvolve developed a more efficient scheduling algorithm for data centers,
found a functionally equivalent simplification in the circuit design of
hardware accelerators, and accelerated the training of the LLM underpinning
AlphaEvolve itself. Furthermore, AlphaEvolve discovered novel, provably correct
algorithms that surpass state-of-the-art solutions on a spectrum of problems in
mathematics and computer science, significantly expanding the scope of prior
automated discovery methods (Romera-Paredes et al., 2023). Notably, AlphaEvolve
developed a search algorithm that found a procedure to multiply two $4 \times
4$ complex-valued matrices using $48$ scalar multiplications; offering the
first improvement, after 56 years, over Strassen's algorithm in this setting.
We believe AlphaEvolve and coding agents like it can have a significant impact
in improving solutions of problems across many areas of science and
computation.

keyInsights: Integrating Large Language Models (LLMs) into an evolutionary feedback loop allows for continuous, iterative refinement of code, transforming LLMs from one-shot generators into active agents for discovery.
LLMs can automate the design of complex 'evolution operators' and even evolve entire heuristic search algorithms, enabling adaptive, multi-stage problem-solving previously difficult to achieve.

Combining an ensemble of LLMs with efficient, parallelized evaluation cascades and multi-objective optimization strategies significantly boosts the system's ability to explore complex search spaces and identify subtle improvements.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) often struggle to generate entirely novel and verifiable scientific or algorithmic discoveries in a single attempt, frequently producing "hallucinations."
Automated discovery systems need to move beyond simple code generation to enable continuous, iterative improvement of complex algorithms based on empirical feedback.

Optimizing critical computational infrastructure and solving open scientific problems often require designing sophisticated, multi-stage search algorithms or making subtle, high-impact code changes that are difficult for humans or prior AI systems to identify.
results: Discovered a procedure for multiplying 4x4 complex matrices using 48 scalar multiplications, improving upon Strassen’s 49-multiplication algorithm (a first in 56 years) and enhancing 14 other matrix multiplication algorithms.
Achieved new state-of-the-art results in approximately 20% of over 50 open mathematical problems, including improving the lower bound for the kissing number in 11 dimensions from 592 to 593.

Deployed optimizations across Google's computing ecosystem, recovering 0.7% of fleet-wide compute resources in data center scheduling, accelerating Gemini training kernels by 23%, and contributing to TPU hardware circuit design.
solution: AlphaEvolve implements a distributed, asynchronous evolutionary pipeline that orchestrates state-of-the-art Large Language Models (LLMs) within a continuous feedback loop.
LLMs are prompted to propose targeted code modifications (diff blocks) to existing programs, which are then automatically evaluated against user-defined metrics.

The system maintains a 'Program database' inspired by MAP-elites to balance exploration and exploitation, iteratively refining solutions, and utilizes an ensemble of LLMs for high-throughput and high-quality generation.
paper_summary.summary: AlphaEvolve, from Google DeepMind, combines large language models with an evolutionary search framework to autonomously discover novel algorithms and optimize code. This system identified a faster procedure for 4x4 complex matrix multiplication, improved state-of-the-art for several open mathematical problems, and delivered tangible optimizations for Google's computing ecosystem, including recovering 0.7% of fleet-wide compute resources.

title: AlphaEvolve: A coding agent for scientific and algorithmic discovery

abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based evolution is a promising approach for open-ended discovery, where progress requires sustained search and knowledge accumulation. Existing methods still rely heavily on fixed heuristics and hard-coded exploration rules, which limit the autonomy of LLM agents. We present CORAL, the first framework for autonomous multi-agent evolution on open-ended problems. CORAL replaces rigid control with long-running agents that explore, reflect, and collaborate through shared persistent memory, asynchronous multi-agent execution, and heartbeat-based interventions. It also provides practical safeguards, including isolated workspaces, evaluator separation, resource management, and agent session and health management. Evaluated on diverse mathematical, algorithmic, and systems optimization tasks, CORAL sets new state-of-the-art results on 10 tasks, achieving 3-10 times higher improvement rates with far fewer evaluations than fixed evolutionary search baselines across tasks. On Anthropic's kernel engineering task, four co-evolving agents improve the best known score from 1363 to 1103 cycles. Mechanistic analyses further show how these gains arise from knowledge reuse and multi-agent exploration and communication. Together, these results suggest that greater agent autonomy and multi-agent evolution can substantially improve open-ended discovery. Code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Granting LLM agents autonomy over evolutionary decisions and facilitating local verification steps leads to substantially more efficient search and higher improvement rates compared to fixed evolutionary algorithms.

Asynchronous multi-agent organization with shared persistent memory fosters emergent collaboration and diverse exploration, extending the search frontier beyond what single autonomous agents can achieve.

The systematic accumulation and sharing of knowledge, via agent-generated notes and reusable skills, is critical for driving successful and sustained discovery, especially in complex open-ended tasks.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-powered evolutionary search relies on fixed heuristics and external rules, limiting agent autonomy and adaptability in complex, open-ended problem spaces where optimal search strategies are unknown.

Current multi-agent LLM systems often use 'vertical scaling' with human-defined roles and rigid communication, which is restrictive for truly open-ended discovery where optimal task decomposition and interaction patterns are not known in advance.
results: A single CORAL agent outperformed fixed evolutionary search baselines on all 11 mathematical and systems optimization tasks, achieving new state-of-the-art results on 8 tasks and demonstrating a 3-10x higher improvement rate.

Multi-agent CORAL (4 agents) extended search performance on challenging stress-test problems, achieving an 18.3% cycle reduction in kernel engineering (a new SOTA) and a 5.0% score increase in polyominoes packing.

Analysis revealed that multi-agent gains stem from cross-agent information transfer (e.g., 36% of attempts on Kernel Engineering used cross-agent parents with higher improvement rates) and increased exploration diversity (low Jaccard similarity of strategies across agents).
solution: CORAL implements autonomous multi-agent evolution by empowering LLM agents with control over the retrieve, propose, evaluate, and update stages of the evolutionary loop, rather than relying on fixed external rules.

A shared persistent memory, structured as a file system, enables asynchronous multi-agent organization where agents explore solution spaces in parallel and indirectly exchange discoveries, notes, and reusable skills.

Heartbeat-based interventions (e.g., periodic consolidation, stagnation redirection) are incorporated to prevent agents from getting stuck, encourage knowledge contribution, and dynamically reorient search strategies.
paper_summary.summary: The CORAL framework introduces an autonomous multi-agent evolutionary system, empowering Large Language Model agents to control their discovery process and collaborate indirectly through shared persistent memory. This approach achieved new state-of-the-art results on 8 out of 11 mathematical and systems optimization tasks, exhibiting a 3-10x higher improvement rate and an 18.3% cycle reduction in kernel engineering.

title: CORAL: Towards Autonomous Multi-Agent Evolution for Open-Ended Discovery

abstract: The development of native computer-use agents (CUA) represents a significant leap in multimodal AI. However, their potential is currently bottlenecked by the constraints of static data scaling. Existing paradigms relying primarily on passive imitation of static datasets struggle to capture the intricate causal dynamics inherent in long-horizon computer tasks. In this work, we introduce EvoCUA, a native computer use agentic model. Unlike static imitation, EvoCUA integrates data generation and policy optimization into a self-sustaining evolutionary cycle. To mitigate data scarcity, we develop a verifiable synthesis engine that autonomously generates diverse tasks coupled with executable validators. To enable large-scale experience acquisition, we design a scalable infrastructure orchestrating tens of thousands of asynchronous sandbox rollouts. Building on these massive trajectories, we propose an iterative evolving learning strategy to efficiently internalize this experience. This mechanism dynamically regulates policy updates by identifying capability boundaries -- reinforcing successful routines while transforming failure trajectories into rich supervision through error analysis and self-correction. Empirical evaluations on the OSWorld benchmark demonstrate that EvoCUA achieves a success rate of 56.7%, establishing a new open-source state-of-the-art. Notably, EvoCUA significantly outperforms the previous best open-source model, OpenCUA-72B (45.0%), and surpasses leading closed-weights models such as UI-TARS-2 (53.1%). Crucially, our results underscore the generalizability of this approach: the evolving paradigm driven by learning from experience yields consistent performance gains across foundation models of varying scales, establishing a robust and scalable path for advancing native agent capabilities.

keyInsights: Shifting from static data scaling to 'experience scaling' via massive interactive rollouts is crucial for developing robust and generalizable computer-use agents.

Both successful and failed trajectories offer distinct, valuable supervisory signals: successes require aggressive denoising, while failures provide high-information insights for error analysis and preference construction.

A comprehensively defined native action space, a structured thought space with reasoning schemas, and an iterative training approach with on-policy data are fundamental for efficient capability evolution.
originalProblem: Computer-use agents (CUAs) traditionally rely on static imitation learning from fixed datasets, which limits their ability to capture complex causal dynamics and long-horizon planning in GUIs.
Manual annotation of high-quality, diverse, and verifiable interaction data for GUI agents is prohibitively expensive, leading to data scarcity and quality issues.
Scaling interactive learning for CUAs lacks robust infrastructure and efficient training strategies to manage the computational costs and complexity of large-scale interaction spaces.
results: EvoCUA-32B achieved a 56.7% success rate on the OSWorld benchmark, setting a new open-source state-of-the-art and outperforming the previous best open-source model, OpenCUA-72B (45.0%), by +11.7%.
The model is competitive with closed-weights models, surpassing UI-TARS-2-2509 (53.1%) by +3.6% and narrowing the gap with Claude-4.5-Sonnet (58.1%) to just 1.4% under equivalent step constraints.
Ablation studies demonstrated monotonic improvements from each component, with the unified action space contributing +4.84%, Cold Start +2.62%, Rejection Fine-Tuning +3.13%, and Offline DPO +3.21%.
solution: EvoCUA employs a Verifiable Synthesis Engine to autonomously generate diverse tasks coupled with executable validators, ensuring strict environmental grounding and quality.
It utilizes a Scalable Interaction Infrastructure, built on a hybrid virtualization architecture, for high-throughput orchestration and massive interactive experience production.

An Evolving Paradigm via Learning from Experience iteratively refines the agent's policy through a cold-start initialization with behavioral priors, rejection sampling fine-tuning (RFT) for successes, and step-level Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for rectifying failures.
paper_summary.summary: Meituan, in collaboration with Fudan, Tongji, and HKUST, developed EvoCUA, an evolutionary framework for computer-use agents that learns from scalable synthetic experience instead of static datasets. This approach achieved a 56.7% success rate on the OSWorld benchmark, establishing a new open-source state-of-the-art and nearing the performance of leading closed-source models.

title: EvoCUA: Evolving Computer Use Agents via Learning from Scalable Synthetic Experience

abstract: Open-ended self-improving agents can autonomously modify their own structural designs to advance their capabilities and overcome the limits of pre-defined architectures, thus reducing reliance on human intervention. We introduce Group-Evolving Agents (GEA), a new paradigm for open-ended self-improvements, which treats a group of agents as the fundamental evolutionary unit, enabling explicit experience sharing and reuse within the group throughout evolution. Unlike existing open-ended self-evolving paradigms that adopt tree-structured evolution, GEA overcomes the limitation of inefficient utilization of exploratory diversity caused by isolated evolutionary branches. We evaluate GEA on challenging coding benchmarks, where it significantly outperforms state-of-the-art self-evolving methods (71.0% vs. 56.7% on SWE-bench Verified, 88.3% vs. 68.3% on Polyglot) and matches or exceeds top human-designed agent frameworks (71.8% and 52.0% on two benchmarks, respectively). Analysis reveals that GEA more effectively converts early-stage exploratory diversity into sustained, long-term progress, achieving stronger performance under the same number of evolved agents. Furthermore, GEA exhibits consistent transferability across different coding models and greater robustness, fixing framework-level bugs in 1.4 iterations on average, versus 5 for self-evolving methods.

keyInsights: Treating a group of agents as the evolutionary unit, with explicit experience sharing, transforms transient exploratory diversity into sustained, cumulative progress for AI self-improvement.
AI agents, unlike biological organisms, are not bound by lineage and can directly share and aggregate 'trajectories, tools, and learned artifacts,' leading to more efficient evolution.

Meta-learning based self-improvement allows agents to autonomously evolve their own framework-level designs, matching or exceeding the performance of human-designed systems without direct human intervention.
originalProblem: Traditional AI systems rely on pre-defined architectures and continuous human intervention, limiting their ability for autonomous, open-ended self-improvement.

Existing open-ended self-evolving AI, often inspired by biological evolution, uses individual-centric, tree-structured approaches that isolate evolutionary branches, preventing effective experience sharing and long-term cumulative progress.
Exploratory diversity generated by individual agents in isolation often remains transient, failing to contribute to the collective and sustained advancement of AI capabilities.
results: GEA achieved a 71.0% success rate on SWE-bench Verified and 88.3% on Polyglot, significantly outperforming the state-of-the-art individual-centric Darwin Gödel Machine (DGM) baseline (56.7% and 68.3% respectively).
The autonomously evolved GEA agents demonstrated performance competitive with or superior to human-designed state-of-the-art frameworks, such as OpenHands + GPT-5 (71.8%) and Aider + GPT-5 (52.0%).

GEA exhibited enhanced robustness by repairing framework-level bugs in an average of 1.4 iterations, faster than DGM's 5 iterations, and demonstrated transferability of improvements across different backbone models (e.g., GPT and Claude series).
solution: GEA introduces a group-centric evolutionary paradigm where a collection of agents, rather than individuals, forms the fundamental unit of evolution.
The framework incorporates a Parent Group Selection mechanism that selects a diverse, high-performing group of agents using a balanced performance-novelty score.

Open-Ended Group Evolution explicitly enables intra-group experience sharing, aggregating all evolutionary traces from the parent group into a shared pool for every agent to utilize in its reflection and evolution modules.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the University of California, Santa Barbara developed Group-Evolving Agents (GEA), a framework enabling open-ended self-improvement for AI systems by evolving groups of agents that share experiences. This group-centric approach led to a 71.0% success rate on SWE-bench Verified and 88.3% on Polyglot, surpassing individual-centric self-evolving methods and matching human-designed agent performance.

title: Group-Evolving Agents: Open-Ended Self-Improvement via Experience Sharing

abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) demonstrate their effectiveness in scaling test-time compute for software engineering tasks. However, these approaches often focus on high-level solutions, with limited attention to optimizing low-level CUDA kernel implementations. Additionally, existing kernel generation benchmarks suffer from exploitable loopholes and insufficient diversity in testing conditions, hindering true generalization assessment. To address these limitations, we introduce robust-kbench, a new benchmark for rigorous evaluation of kernel performance and correctness across varied scenarios. Furthermore, we present a comprehensive agentic framework that automates CUDA kernel discovery, verification, and optimization. This pipeline enables frontier LLMs to translate torch code to CUDA kernels and iteratively improve their runtime within our robust evaluation setting. Our sequential workflow first translates PyTorch code into equivalent CUDA kernels. It then optimizes their runtime using a novel evolutionary meta-generation procedure tailored to the CUDA ecosystem, guided by LLM-based verifiers for correctness and efficient filtering. Evaluated on robust-kbench, our approach produces CUDA kernels outperforming torch implementations for practical applications, including forward and backward passes. It can fuse operations and deploy various runtime optimization strategies. The verifier workflow accurately classifies incorrect kernels, enhancing hardware verification efficiency.

keyInsights: Rigorous benchmarking is essential for evaluating LLM-generated low-level code, as existing benchmarks can lead to artificial performance gains by allowing LLMs to exploit loopholes.

LLMs can be effectively specialized and iteratively tuned to perform "soft-verification," accurately identifying various kernel errors (compilation, memory, numerical) before resource-intensive hardware testing.

An evolutionary optimization approach, combining LLM ensembles, strategic in-context learning (least-to-most), and LLM-summarized profiling feedback, consistently improves CUDA kernel runtime performance and robustness.
originalProblem: Modern AI models, especially large foundation models, demand increasingly efficient low-level GPU operations, but expertise in writing optimized CUDA kernels is scarce.
Current LLM applications for code generation often lack the ability to produce truly efficient, hardware-specific implementations for tasks like CUDA kernels.
Existing CUDA kernel benchmarks suffer from exploitable loopholes and insufficient diversity, preventing accurate assessment and robust optimization of LLM-generated code.
results: Re-evaluation of `KernelBench v0` tasks, after filtering out contaminated examples, reduced reported LLM speedups from an average of 3.13x to 1.49x, validating the need for robust benchmarks.
LLM-based soft-verifiers achieved up to 82% accuracy in error detection, increasing the proportion of valid kernels in the optimization pipeline from 55-70% to 80-85% by pre-filtering errors.

The agentic framework discovered CUDA kernels that achieved up to 2.5x speedup in the forward pass over PyTorch eager baselines, with LayerNorm showing a 12.52x speedup and LlamaRMSNorm a 3.93x speedup.
solution: A robust benchmark, "robust-kbench," was created with diverse testing conditions, comprehensive forward/backward pass evaluation, and multi-faceted profiling to rigorously assess kernel performance.

An LLM-based soft-verification workflow uses specialized verifiers, refined through iterative prompt tuning, to pre-screen generated kernels for correctness errors, significantly reducing hardware testing needs.

An agentic, evolutionary optimization framework iteratively translates PyTorch code to CUDA, then refines kernels using an ensemble of LLMs, in-context examples of high-performing code, and LLM-summarized profiling feedback.
paper_summary.summary: Sakana AI researchers developed an agentic framework that employs large language models to automate the discovery, soft-verification, and iterative optimization of low-level CUDA kernels. This system generated kernels that achieved up to 2.5x speedup over PyTorch eager baselines for forward passes, improved robustness by pre-filtering errors, and demonstrated generalization across various deep learning tasks.

title: Towards Robust Agentic CUDA Kernel Benchmarking, Verification, and Optimization

abstract: The paradigm of automated program generation is shifting from one-shot generation to inference-time search, where Large Language Models (LLMs) function as semantic mutation operators within evolutionary loops. While effective, these systems are currently governed by static schedules that fail to account for the non-stationary dynamics of the search process. This rigidity results in substantial computational waste, as resources are indiscriminately allocated to stagnating populations while promising frontiers remain under-exploited. We introduce AdaEvolve, a framework that reformulates LLM-driven evolution as a hierarchical adaptive optimization problem. AdaEvolve uses an "accumulated improvement signal" to unify decisions across three levels: Local Adaptation, which dynamically modulates the exploration intensity within a population of solution candidates; Global Adaptation, which routes the global resource budget via bandit-based scheduling across different solution candidate populations; and Meta-Guidance which generates novel solution tactics based on the previously generated solutions and their corresponding improvements when the progress stalls. We demonstrate that AdaEvolve consistently outperforms the open-sourced baselines across 185 different open-ended optimization problems including combinatorial, systems optimization and algorithm design problems.

keyInsights: An "accumulated improvement signal," reflecting the productivity of the search trajectory, can effectively drive multi-level adaptive control in zeroth-order optimization.

Hierarchical adaptation, encompassing local search intensity, global resource allocation, and meta-level strategy shifts, is crucial for breaking both numerical and conceptual local optima in complex search spaces.
Globally normalizing bandit rewards for island selection prevents bias towards trivial improvements on low-performing islands, ensuring resources are directed towards globally significant progress.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-guided search systems use static schedules and fixed hyperparameters, leading to computational waste and premature stagnation in discrete, non-differentiable search spaces.
These systems lack adaptability to the non-stationary dynamics of evolutionary search, often getting trapped in local optima and requiring extensive manual tuning for each new problem.
The semantic capabilities of LLMs are underutilized for overarching search control and strategic redirection beyond simple program mutations.
results: AdaEvolve consistently outperformed open-sourced baselines (OpenEvolve, GEPA, ShinkaEvolve) across all categories of 185 diverse problems, including mathematical optimization, systems benchmarks, and Frontier-CS tasks.
It achieved state-of-the-art results in 4 of 6 mathematical optimization problems, notably surpassing human SOTA on Circle Packing (Square, N=26) with a score of 2.636 (vs. 2.634).

Comprehensive ablation studies confirmed the individual contributions of all three adaptive control levels, with meta-guidance proving most impactful for enabling breakthroughs by breaking conceptual stalemates.
solution: AdaEvolve introduces a hierarchical adaptive optimization framework that uses an "accumulated improvement signal" to unify and coordinate adaptation across three levels of control.

It dynamically modulates exploration/exploitation within islands, allocates global computational resources to the most productive islands via a multi-armed bandit approach with globally normalized rewards, and spawns new islands during stagnation.
A "System 2" meta-LLM is triggered when global stagnation persists to generate high-level "solution tactics" which are then injected into mutation prompts to shift search strategy.
paper_summary.summary: AdaEvolve, an adaptive framework developed at UC Berkeley, reframes LLM-driven program evolution as a hierarchical optimization problem, employing an "accumulated improvement signal" to dynamically manage search processes. The framework achieved state-of-the-art results on 4 out of 6 mathematical optimization tasks, surpassing human SOTA on Circle Packing and consistently outperforming baselines across 185 diverse problems.

title: AdaEvolve: Adaptive LLM Driven Zeroth-Order Optimization

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have recently shown strong coding abilities, enabling not only static code generation but also iterative code self-evolving through agentic frameworks. Recently, AlphaEvolve \cite{novikov2025alphaevolve} demonstrated that LLM-based coding agents can autonomously improve algorithms and surpass human experts, with scopes limited to isolated kernels spanning hundreds of lines of code. Inspired by AlphaEvolve, we present SATLUTION, the first framework to extend LLM-based code evolution to the full repository scale, encompassing hundreds of files and tens of thousands of lines of C/C++ code. Targeting Boolean Satisfiability (SAT), the canonical NP-complete problem and a cornerstone of both theory and applications. SATLUTION orchestrates LLM agents to directly evolve solver repositories under strict correctness guarantees and distributed runtime feedback, while simultaneously self-evolving its own evolution policies and rules. Starting from SAT Competition 2024 codebases and benchmark, SATLUTION evolved solvers that decisively outperformed the human-designed winners of the SAT Competition 2025, and also surpassed both 2024 and 2025 champions on the 2024 benchmarks.

keyInsights: LLM agents can effectively manage and evolve complex software at a full repository scale, moving beyond small algorithmic kernels.
A self-improving rule system, integrating both static foundational knowledge and dynamically learned constraints, is crucial for stable and efficient autonomous code evolution.
Rigorous, domain-specific correctness verification, particularly through an indispensable manually engineered verifier, is foundational for reliable AI-driven development in high-stakes applications.
A semi-automated operational model, blending AI automation with targeted human intervention for conceptual guidance, proved most effective for highly complex tasks.
originalProblem: Scaling LLM-based code evolution beyond isolated code snippets to manage and improve entire, complex software repositories.
Addressing the diminishing returns and high expertise barrier in the manual, intuition-driven development of state-of-the-art combinatorial optimization algorithms like SAT solvers.
Harnessing LLM capabilities for algorithmic discovery and innovation in computationally challenging, NP-complete problems while ensuring strict correctness guarantees.
results: SATLUTION's evolved SAT solvers outperformed all human-designed entrants in the SAT Competition 2025, solving more instances (e.g., 347 vs. 334 for the 2025 gold medalist) with lower PAR-2 scores on unseen benchmarks.

The self-evolution process demonstrated consistent performance improvement over approximately 70 iterations, significantly surpassing initial state-of-the-art baselines on both 2024 and 2025 benchmarks.
The autonomous evolution led to the discovery and refinement of various solver-design insights and techniques, cumulatively expanding the solver repository by over 10,000 lines of C/C++ code.

The total computational cost was less than $20,000 (including LLM token usage and distributed evaluation), demonstrating a cost-effective approach compared to human expert effort for similar advancements.
solution:
Designed SATLUTION, an autonomous agent-based framework utilizing Planning and Coding LLM agents within an iterative Generate 
 Compile 
 Test 
 Analyze & Reason 
 Evolve loop.
Implemented a hybrid rule system combining initial static domain knowledge with dynamic, self-evolving rules that adapt to observed failure patterns and refine guidance over time.
Incorporated a stringent two-stage verification pipeline (compilation/smoke tests and full correctness validation with DRAT proof checking) to guarantee the functional soundness of evolved solvers.
paper_summary.summary: NVIDIA Research developed SATLUTION, an LLM-based framework that autonomously evolves complex Boolean Satisfiability (SAT) solvers at a full repository scale, encompassing tens of thousands of lines of C/C++ code. The evolved solvers surpassed human-designed champions from the SAT Competition 2025, achieving lower penalized average runtime (PAR-2) scores and solving more instances on unseen benchmarks.

title: Autonomous Code Evolution Meets NP-Completeness

abstract: GPU kernel optimization has long been a central challenge at the intersection of high-performance computing and machine learning. Efficient kernels are crucial for accelerating large language model (LLM) training and serving, yet attaining high performance typically requires extensive manual tuning. Compiler-based systems reduce some of this burden, but still demand substantial manual design and engineering effort. Recently, researchers have explored using LLMs for GPU kernel generation, though prior work has largely focused on translating high-level PyTorch modules into CUDA code. In this work, we introduce Astra, the first LLM-based multi-agent system for GPU kernel optimization. Unlike previous approaches, Astra starts from existing CUDA implementations extracted from SGLang, a widely deployed framework for serving LLMs, rather than treating PyTorch modules as the specification. Within Astra, specialized LLM agents collaborate through iterative code generation, testing, profiling, and planning to produce kernels that are both correct and high-performance. On kernels from SGLang, Astra achieves an average speedup of 1.32x using zero-shot prompting with OpenAI o4-mini. A detailed case study further demonstrates that LLMs can autonomously apply loop transformations, optimize memory access patterns, exploit CUDA intrinsics, and leverage fast math operations to yield substantial performance gains. Our work highlights multi-agent LLM systems as a promising new paradigm for GPU kernel optimization. Our code is publicly available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Decomposing the complex GPU kernel optimization task into specialized roles for distinct LLM agents significantly enhances the system's ability to systematically explore the optimization space.
LLMs, when orchestrated within a multi-agent framework, can autonomously identify and apply sophisticated low-level GPU optimizations that traditionally require expert human insight.
An iterative feedback loop, powered by dedicated testing and profiling agents, is crucial for guiding the planning and coding agents to achieve performance gains while preserving correctness.
originalProblem: Manual GPU kernel optimization is highly labor-intensive, time-consuming, and difficult to adapt to evolving hardware and model architectures.
Traditional compiler-based optimization systems require substantial engineering effort and often fall short of expert-level performance or generalization across diverse hardware.
The multi-faceted nature of GPU kernel optimization (code generation, testing, profiling, strategic planning) proves challenging for single, monolithic LLM agents.
results: Astra achieved an average speedup of 1.32x across three production-grade CUDA kernels from the SGLang framework, with individual kernel speedups ranging from 1.25x to 1.46x.
All optimized kernels generated by Astra were rigorously validated to be functionally correct, demonstrating the system's reliability.
The multi-agent approach significantly outperformed a single-agent baseline, which yielded an average speedup of only 1.08x and could lead to performance degradations for more complex kernels.
solution: Astra introduces a multi-agent system where specialized LLM agents collaborate in an iterative feedback loop to optimize existing CUDA kernels.

Four distinct agents (Testing, Profiling, Planning, and Coding) are responsible for generating test cases, measuring performance, strategizing optimizations, and implementing code changes, respectively.
The system operates in a zero-shot prompting setup using OpenAI's o4-mini model over five optimization rounds.
paper_summary.summary: Astra, a multi-agent system, autonomously optimizes existing GPU kernels from the SGLang framework, achieving an average speedup of 1.32x while maintaining functional correctness. The system leverages specialized LLM agents to apply advanced optimization strategies, demonstrating LLMs' capability in complex, iterative performance tuning.

title: Astra: A Multi-Agent System for GPU Kernel Performance Optimization

abstract: Hardware design automation faces challenges in generating high-quality Verilog code efficiently. This paper introduces VFlow, an automated framework that optimizes agentic workflows for Verilog code generation. Unlike traditional approaches relying on fixed prompts or manually designed flows, VFlow treats workflow discovery as a search over graph-structured LLM invocation sequences. It introduces a multi-population cooperative evolution (CEPE-MCTS) algorithm that balances multiple hardware objectives -- functional correctness, area, power, timing and token cost -- while sharing successful patterns and avoiding repeated failures. Integrated multi-level verification ensures syntactic correctness, functional behavior, and synthesizability. Experiments on VerilogEval and RTLLM2.0 show VFlow improves pass@1 by 20--30\% over prompting baselines and closely matches designer-level area/power. Remarkably, VFlow enables small LLMs to outperform larger models with up to 10.9$\times$ ROI, offering a cost-effective solution for RTL design. This work paves the way for intelligent, automated hardware development, advancing LLM applications in EDA.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China
Ningbo Institute of Digital Twin, Eastern Institute of Technology

keyInsights: Decomposing multi-objective hardware design into specialized search populations within an MCTS framework effectively addresses objective conflicts and enhances search efficiency.
Directly embedding multi-level, domain-specific verification into the workflow optimization loop is crucial for generating high-quality, synthesizable, and robust Verilog code.

Mechanisms like fragment migration and global failure experience sharing significantly accelerate the discovery of optimal workflows by propagating effective design patterns and preventing recurrent errors.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based Verilog generation methods rely on fixed prompts or manual workflows, hindering efficient exploration of the complex hardware design space.

Current approaches struggle to systematically optimize for multiple conflicting hardware objectives (e.g., functional correctness, area, power, timing) and fully integrate multi-level verification processes.
The high computational cost of large general-purpose LLMs makes them less practical for widespread application in hardware design automation tasks.
results: VFlow achieved the highest pass@1 rates on VerilogEval benchmarks (84.3% for machine, 82.8% for human), representing average improvements of +19.6% and +30.9% respectively over direct LLM invocation.

The framework consistently matched or surpassed best baselines in hardware metrics for RTLLM2.0 modules, producing area and power values comparable to designer references (e.g., 68 μm² area and 6.6 μW power for the "mux" module).

VFlow enabled smaller LLMs (e.g., DeepSeek-V3) to outperform larger models (GPT-4o baseline) in pass@1 rates (80.5% vs. 57.0%), while reducing token consumption by 0.4x and cost by 0.13x, achieving a 10.9x ROI.
solution: VFlow formulates Verilog generation as a search problem over agentic workflows, represented as graphs of LLM invocations, optimized by a Cooperative Evolution with Past Experience Monte Carlo Tree Search (CEPE-MCTS) algorithm.
CEPE-MCTS employs a multi-population cooperative architecture where specialized populations concurrently optimize for distinct hardware objectives (functionality, area, timing, balanced).

The framework integrates domain-specific verification operators for syntax, functional simulation, and circuit optimization, complemented by fragment migration for knowledge sharing and a global failure experience repository.
paper_summary.summary: VFlow presents an automated framework that leverages Monte Carlo Tree Search to discover optimal agentic workflows for generating Verilog code. This system achieves superior functional correctness on VerilogEval benchmarks and optimizes hardware metrics, enabling smaller LLMs to outperform larger models with reduced computational costs.

title: VFlow: Discovering Optimal Agentic Workflows for Verilog Generation

abstract: Recent work such as AlphaEvolve has shown that combining LLM-driven optimization with evolutionary search can effectively improve programs, prompts, and algorithms across domains. In this paradigm, previously evaluated solutions are reused to guide the model toward new candidate solutions. Crucially, the effectiveness of this evolution process depends on the search strategy: how prior solutions are selected and varied to generate new candidates. However, most existing methods rely on fixed search strategies with predefined knobs (e.g., explore-exploit ratios) that remain static throughout execution. While effective in some settings, these approaches often fail to adapt across tasks, or even within the same task as the search space changes over time. We introduce EvoX, an adaptive evolution method that optimizes its own evolution process. EvoX jointly evolves candidate solutions and the search strategies used to generate them, continuously updating how prior solutions are selected and varied based on progress. This enables the system to dynamically shift between different search strategies during the optimization process. Across nearly 200 real-world optimization tasks, EvoX outperforms existing AI-driven evolutionary methods including AlphaEvolve, OpenEvolve, GEPA, and ShinkaEvolve on the majority of tasks.

keyInsights: Treating the search strategy itself as an evolvable entity, rather than a fixed component, significantly enhances the adaptability and effectiveness of LLM-driven optimization.
Dynamic adaptation of search strategies, conditioned on the current state of the solution population, allows for overcoming stagnation and maintaining progress across varied optimization stages.
The meta-evolutionary approach enables the system to discover fundamentally new optimization mechanisms and problem-solving paradigms, moving beyond incremental improvements.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-driven evolutionary systems rely on fixed search strategies that struggle to adapt across diverse tasks or as the optimization landscape evolves.
The inability of static strategies to adapt within a single optimization run leads to stagnation and suboptimal performance.
Manual tuning of search strategy parameters requires significant expert knowledge and effort, limiting the autonomy and scalability of such systems.
results: EvoX consistently outperformed existing AI-driven evolutionary methods (AlphaEvolve, OpenEvolve, GEPA, ShinkaEvolve) on the majority of 196 real-world tasks, often matching or exceeding human-designed solutions within a fixed evaluation budget.
On mathematical optimization, EvoX (with Gemini-3.0-Pro) achieved the best results on all 8 tasks and 7 of 8 mean scores; in system performance, it exceeded human-best results on all 6 benchmarks.

Ablation studies showed EvoX's robustness to initialization, its cost-efficiency (less than $1 for significant progress on Heilbronn triangle), and its ability to dynamically shift strategies to break plateaus and achieve higher peak scores.
solution: EvoX introduces a two-level evolutionary process with an inner loop for evolving solutions and an outer loop for meta-evolving the search strategy itself.

It dynamically generates and updates search strategies (parent selection, variation operators, inspiration sets) based on observed progress and a comprehensive descriptor of the current solution population state.

A strategy efficacy score and stagnation detection mechanism trigger the meta-evolution loop, allowing the system to adapt its approach (e.g., shifting from local refinement to structural variation) when progress slows.
paper_summary.summary: EvoX is a meta-evolutionary framework that enables LLM-driven optimization systems to dynamically adapt and improve their search strategies over time, rather than relying on fixed heuristics. It consistently outperformed existing methods across 196 diverse tasks, discovering novel algorithms and improving performance in mathematics, systems, and algorithmic challenges.

title: EvoX: Meta-Evolution for Automated Discovery

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are catalyzing the development of autonomous AI research agents for scientific and engineering discovery. We present FM Agent, a novel and general-purpose multi-agent framework that leverages a synergistic combination of LLM-based reasoning and large-scale evolutionary search to address complex real-world challenges. The core of FM Agent integrates several key innovations: 1) a cold-start initialization phase incorporating expert guidance, 2) a novel evolutionary sampling strategy for iterative optimization, 3) domain-specific evaluators that combine correctness, effectiveness, and LLM-supervised feedback, and 4) a distributed, asynchronous execution infrastructure built on Ray. Demonstrating broad applicability, our system has been evaluated across diverse domains, including operations research, machine learning, GPU kernel optimization, and classical mathematical problems. FM Agent reaches state-of-the-art results autonomously, without human interpretation or tuning -- 1976.3 on ALE-Bench (+5.2\%), 43.56\% on MLE-Bench (+4.0pp), up to 20x speedups on KernelBench, and establishes new state-of-the-art(SOTA) results on several classical mathematical problems. Beyond academic benchmarks, FM Agent shows considerable promise for both large-scale enterprise R\&D workflows and fundamental scientific research, where it can accelerate innovation, automate complex discovery processes, and deliver substantial engineering and scientific advances with broader societal impact.
title: The FM Agent

abstract:
The past two years have witnessed the evolution of large language model
(LLM)-based multi-agent systems from labor-intensive manual design to partial
automation (\textit{e.g.}, prompt engineering, communication topology) and
eventually to fully automated design. However, existing agentic automation
pipelines often lack LLM heterogeneity and focus on single-objective
performance optimization, limiting their potential to combine weaker models for
more customized and cost-effective solutions. To address this challenge, we
propose EvoFlow, a niching evolutionary algorithm-based framework to
automatically search a population of heterogeneous and complexity-adaptive
agentic workflows, rather than a single homogeneous, complex workflow.
Technically, EvoFlow performs \textit{(1) tag-based retrieval} to extract
parent workflows from an agentic population, evolves new workflows through
\textit{(2) crossover} and \textit{(3) mutation}, and employs \textit{(4)
niching-based selection} to maintain population diversity and quality.
Extensive evaluations across seven benchmarks demonstrate that EvoFlow is:
\textbf{(I) diverse}, evolving a population of workflows ranging from simple
I/O tasks to complex multi-turn interactions; \textbf{(II) high-performing},
outperforming previous handcrafted and automated workflows by
$1.23\%\sim29.86\%$; \textbf{(III) economical}, surpassing powerful
\llmname{o1-preview} at $12.4\%$ of its inference cost using weaker open-source
models.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Multi-objective optimization balancing cost and performance enables more efficient resource utilization
Maintaining workflow diversity through niching helps address varying task complexities
Heterogeneous LLM combinations can achieve better results than single high-end models
originalProblem: Existing multi-agent systems rely on manual workflow design and homogeneous LLMs, leading to high costs and limited adaptability
Current approaches lack mechanisms to balance performance and cost while maintaining workflow diversity across varying task complexities
results: Outperforms previous approaches by 1.23%~29.86% across seven benchmarks
Achieves better performance than GPT-4 while reducing costs by 87.6%
Successfully evolves workflows ranging from simple I/O to complex multi-turn interactions
Demonstrates effective cross-domain optimization and complexity adaptation
solution: Automated evolution of workflows using tag-based retrieval, crossover/mutation operations, and niching-based selection
Hierarchical search space combining invoking nodes, operator nodes, and complete workflows with heterogeneous LLM integration
paper_summary.summary: EvoFlow introduces a groundbreaking evolutionary framework that automatically designs diverse and adaptive multi-agent workflows, achieving superior performance to GPT-4 at just 12.4% of the cost through innovative combination of heterogeneous language models and multi-objective optimization techniques developed by researchers from leading Chinese institutions.

title: EvoFlow: Evolving Diverse Agentic Workflows On The Fly

abstract: Optimizing GPU kernels is critical for efficient modern machine learning systems yet remains challenging due to the complex interplay of design factors and rapid hardware evolution. Existing automated approaches typically treat Large Language Models (LLMs) merely as stochastic code generators within heuristic-guided evolutionary loops. These methods often struggle with complex kernels requiring coordinated, multi-step structural transformations, as they lack explicit planning capabilities and frequently discard promising strategies due to inefficient or incorrect intermediate implementations. To address this, we propose Search via Co-Evolving World Model and build K-Search based on this method. By replacing static search heuristics with a co-evolving world model, our framework leverages LLMs' prior domain knowledge to guide the search, actively exploring the optimization space. This approach explicitly decouples high-level algorithmic planning from low-level program instantiation, enabling the system to navigate non-monotonic optimization paths while remaining resilient to temporary implementation defects. We evaluate K-Search on diverse, complex kernels from FlashInfer, including GQA, MLA, and MoE kernels. Our results show that K-Search significantly outperforms state-of-the-art evolutionary search methods, achieving an average 2.10x improvement and up to a 14.3x gain on complex MoE kernels. On the GPUMode TriMul task, K-Search achieves state-of-the-art performance on H100, reaching 1030us and surpassing both prior evolution and human-designed solutions.

keyInsights: LLMs can function as intrinsic world models, leveraging their latent planning capabilities to reason over complex optimization problems rather than just generating code.

Explicitly separating strategic planning from tactical code implementation allows for the exploration of non-monotonic optimization paths, where intermediate steps may not yield immediate performance gains.

Dynamic adaptation of the search strategy through a co-evolving world model enhances the robustness of the optimization process, making it less susceptible to transient implementation defects and more efficient in resource allocation.
originalProblem: Optimizing GPU kernels manually is challenging due to vast design spaces, rapid hardware evolution, and the high computational cost of trial-and-error.
Existing LLM-based automated methods for kernel generation treat LLMs primarily as stochastic code generators, struggling with complex multi-step transformations and non-monotonic optimization paths.
These methods are susceptible to implementation defects, potentially discarding theoretically sound strategies due to transient errors in code instantiation, wasting search budget.
results: K-Search consistently and significantly outperformed state-of-the-art evolutionary methods (OpenEvolve, ShinkaEvolve) on FlashInfer kernels, achieving an average final score 2.10x to 2.21x higher.
On specific kernels, K-Search delivered substantial improvements, including a 14.3x gain over OpenEvolve for the MoE kernel and a 5.10x gain over ShinkaEvolve for MLA Prefill.
K-Search achieved a geometric-mean latency of 1030 μs on NVIDIA H100 for the GPUMode TriMul task, surpassing all prior submissions on the official leaderboard, including human-designed solutions.
solution: K-Search repurposes LLMs as intrinsic, co-evolving 'world models' that guide the search process by maintaining an understanding of the kernel design space and actively reasoning over search states.

The framework explicitly decouples high-level algorithmic planning (optimization intent) from low-level program instantiation (code generation) through an iterative three-phase process: action selection, local refinement, and world model co-evolution.

The LLM world model dynamically refines its beliefs and strategies via in-context learning, updating the search frontier, recalibrating priority scores, and pruning unpromising branches using tree edit operations.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at UC Berkeley developed K-Search, a framework for automated GPU kernel optimization that utilizes large language models (LLMs) as co-evolving world models for strategic planning. K-Search generated kernels that consistently outperformed state-of-the-art evolutionary search methods by an average of 2.10x to 2.21x on FlashInfer benchmarks and achieved the best known performance on the GPUMode TriMul task.

title: K-Search: LLM Kernel Generation via Co-Evolving Intrinsic World Model

abstract:
Bayesian optimization (BO) is a powerful class of algorithms for optimizing
expensive black-box functions, but designing effective BO algorithms remains a
manual, expertise-driven task. Recent advancements in Large Language Models
(LLMs) have opened new avenues for automating scientific discovery, including
the automatic design of optimization algorithms. While prior work has used LLMs
within optimization loops or to generate non-BO algorithms, we tackle a new
challenge: Using LLMs to automatically generate full BO algorithm code. Our
framework uses an evolution strategy to guide an LLM in generating Python code
that preserves the key components of BO algorithms: An initial design, a
surrogate model, and an acquisition function. The LLM is prompted to produce
multiple candidate algorithms, which are evaluated on the established Black-Box
Optimization Benchmarking (BBOB) test suite from the COmparing Continuous
Optimizers (COCO) platform. Based on their performance, top candidates are
selected, combined, and mutated via controlled prompt variations, enabling
iterative refinement. Despite no additional fine-tuning, the LLM-generated
algorithms outperform state-of-the-art BO baselines in 19 (out of 24) BBOB
functions in dimension 5 and generalize well to higher dimensions, and
different tasks (from the Bayesmark framework). This work demonstrates that
LLMs can serve as algorithmic co-designers, offering a new paradigm for
automating BO development and accelerating the discovery of novel algorithmic
combinations. The source code is provided at
this https URL

keyInsights: Trust-region mechanisms emerged as a dominant paradigm in top-performing generated algorithms (ATRBO, TREvol), suggesting the evolutionary process effectively discovered adaptive strategies for exploration-exploitation balance
Population-based evolutionary strategies significantly outperformed simple (1+1) configurations due to better recovery from poor generations and enhanced exploration through crossover operations

Generated algorithms demonstrated strong generalization capabilities, maintaining competitive performance on unseen functions and higher dimensions despite being evolved on a subset of 5D BBOB functions
originalProblem: Designing effective Bayesian Optimization algorithms remains a manual, expertise-driven task requiring careful selection and tuning of components like initial design strategies, surrogate models, and acquisition functions

Previous LLM applications in optimization focused on generating metaheuristics or assisting specific BO components, but no framework existed for automatically generating complete BO algorithm implementations
The laborious process of BO algorithm development creates bottlenecks in fields requiring expensive black-box function optimization, limiting accessibility to sophisticated optimization techniques
results: ATRBO achieved superior performance in lower dimensions (d=5, d=10) while TREvol consistently outperformed all state-of-the-art baselines across dimensions d=5-40 on BBOB test suite
Generated algorithms showed competitive performance on 25 real-world ML hyperparameter tuning tasks from Bayesmark framework, demonstrating transferability beyond the training domain
Ablation studies revealed optimal configurations: (4,16) ES with crossover rate 0.6-0.9, temperature 0.5, and top-k 40 for balancing exploration and code quality during evolution
solution: Extend the LLaMEA framework with BO-specific templates, population-based evolutionary strategies, and specialized crossover/mutation operators for evolving complete Python BO algorithms

Use a closed-loop system where gemini-2.0-flash generates BO code, evaluates performance on BBOB functions using Area Over the Convergence Curve metric, and iteratively improves based on fitness feedback
Employ multiple ES configurations (elitist and non-elitist strategies with populations up to 16) to maintain diversity and recover from invalid code generations during the evolutionary process
paper_summary.summary: Leiden University researchers develop LLaMEA-BO, the first framework to automatically generate complete Bayesian Optimization algorithms using large language models guided by evolutionary strategies, where generated algorithms like ATRBO and TREvol consistently match or outperform state-of-the-art baselines (CMA-ES, HEBO, TuRBO1) on BBOB test functions across dimensions 5-40 while demonstrating strong generalization to real-world hyperparameter tuning tasks, establishing LLMs as capable algorithmic co-designers that can autonomously evolve functional Python implementations including initial design, surrogate modeling, and acquisition functions through a closed-loop generate-evaluate-improve process.

title: LLaMEA-BO: A Large Language Model Evolutionary Algorithm for  Automatically Generating Bayesian Optimization Algorithms

abstract:
Agentic AI systems built on large language models (LLMs) offer significant potential for automating complex workflows, from software development to customer support. However, LLM agents often underperform due to suboptimal configurations; poorly tuned prompts, tool descriptions, and parameters that typically require weeks of manual refinement. Existing optimization methods either are too complex for general use or treat components in isolation, missing critical interdependencies.
We present ARTEMIS, a no-code evolutionary optimization platform that jointly optimizes agent configurations through semantically-aware genetic operators. Given only a benchmark script and natural language goals, ARTEMIS automatically discovers configurable components, extracts performance signals from execution logs, and evolves configurations without requiring architectural modifications.
We evaluate ARTEMIS on four representative agent systems: the \emph{ALE Agent} for competitive programming on AtCoder Heuristic Contest, achieving a \textbf{$13.6\%$ improvement} in acceptance rate; the \emph{Mini-SWE Agent} for code optimization on SWE-Perf, with a statistically significant \textbf{10.1\% performance gain}; and the \emph{CrewAI Agent} for cost and mathematical reasoning on Math Odyssey, achieving a statistically significant \textbf{$36.9\%$ reduction} in the number of tokens required for evaluation. We also evaluate the \emph{MathTales-Teacher Agent} powered by a smaller open-source model (Qwen2.5-7B) on GSM8K primary-level mathematics problems, achieving a \textbf{22\% accuracy improvement} and demonstrating that ARTEMIS can optimize agents based on both commercial and local models.

keyInsights: Automated, semantically-aware optimization can discover non-obvious configurations that significantly improve agent performance beyond manual tuning.
The black-box and no-code nature of the Artemis platform democratizes advanced optimization, making it accessible to practitioners without deep expertise in evolutionary algorithms.

While effective, the computational cost of optimization remains high, and the effectiveness is greatest for agents with initial room for improvement or when optimizing secondary objectives like cost efficiency.
originalProblem: Manually tuning LLM-based agent configurations is a time-consuming, non-reproducible, and non-scalable process for expert engineers.
Optimizing agent components in isolation often leads to suboptimal performance due to complex interdependencies between prompts, tools, and parameters.
LLM agent performance cannot typically be optimized with gradient-based methods due to non-differentiable objectives and expensive evaluation costs.
results: The ALE Agent on AtCoder Heuristic Contest showed a 13.6% improvement in acceptance rate (from 66.0% to 75.0%) for prompt optimization.
The Mini-SWE Agent achieved a statistically significant 10.1% performance improvement in code optimization, with project-level gains up to 62% for specific libraries.
The CrewAI Agent on Math Odyssey demonstrated a statistically significant 36.9% decrease in average tokens used, highlighting cost efficiency optimization capabilities.
solution: Artemis uses a general-purpose evolutionary optimization platform to formalize agent optimization as a search problem over mixed-type configuration spaces.
It employs semantic genetic algorithms where LLM ensembles intelligently mutate and crossover natural language components, preserving semantic validity and coherence.
The platform operates as a black-box optimizer, requiring no architectural modifications to the agent, and uses benchmark outcomes for fitness evaluation with hierarchical evaluation strategies.
paper_summary.summary: The Artemis platform automates the optimization of LLM-based agent configurations, encompassing prompts, tool descriptions, and parameters, using semantically-aware genetic algorithms. This approach achieved performance improvements ranging from 9.3% to 36.9% across diverse tasks such as competitive programming, code optimization, and mathematical reasoning, making agent tuning more efficient and accessible.

title: Evolving Excellence: Automated Optimization of LLM-based Agents

abstract: The transition from static Large Language Models (LLMs) to self-improving agents is hindered by the lack of structured reasoning in traditional evolutionary approaches. Existing methods often struggle with premature convergence and inefficient exploration in high-dimensional code spaces. To address these challenges, we introduce LoongFlow, a self-evolving agent framework that achieves state-of-the-art solution quality with significantly reduced computational costs. Unlike "blind" mutation operators, LoongFlow integrates LLMs into a cognitive "Plan-Execute-Summarize" (PES) paradigm, effectively mapping the evolutionary search to a reasoning-heavy process. To sustain long-term architectural coherence, we incorporate a hybrid evolutionary memory system. By synergizing Multi-Island models with MAP-Elites and adaptive Boltzmann selection, this system theoretically balances the exploration-exploitation trade-off, maintaining diverse behavioral niches to prevent optimization stagnation. We instantiate LoongFlow with a General Agent for algorithmic discovery and an ML Agent for pipeline optimization. Extensive evaluations on the AlphaEvolve benchmark and Kaggle competitions demonstrate that LoongFlow outperforms leading baselines (e.g., OpenEvolve, ShinkaEvolve) by up to 60% in evolutionary efficiency while discovering superior solutions. LoongFlow marks a substantial step forward in autonomous scientific discovery, enabling the generation of expert-level solutions with reduced computational overhead.

keyInsights: Integrating LLMs into a cognitive PES paradigm transforms random, blind mutation into a directed, hypothesis-testing evolutionary process, significantly improving search efficiency and solution quality.

A structured feedback loop, enabled by the Summarizer's abductive reflection and stored insights, allows the system to learn from past errors and accumulate "evolutionary wisdom," preventing cyclical mistakes.

A multi-layered hybrid memory system (multi-island, MAP-Elites, adaptive selection) is crucial for maintaining population diversity and preventing premature convergence, leading to a robust and stable search in complex spaces.
originalProblem: Current LLM-based evolutionary optimization methods suffer from inefficient exploration, often relying on high-volume random mutations that lead to computationally expensive "random walk" behavior.
Existing frameworks face diversity collapse and premature convergence to local optima due to a lack of explicit diversity management mechanisms, hindering long-term progress.
These systems typically lack reflexive memory and structured feedback, resulting in "memory-less" searchers that repeatedly make similar errors across generations.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art solution quality on AlphaEvolve Suite problems, e.g., discovering a solution for Autocorrelation II with a score of 0.9027, surpassing AlphaEvolve's 0.8962.

Demonstrated over 60% improvement in evolutionary efficiency, needing an average of 258 evaluations to reach a target score ">=" 0.99 for Circle Packing, compared to OpenEvolve's 783 evaluations, with a 100% success rate.
Secured 14 Gold Medals across various MLEBench competitions and consistently broke theoretical performance barriers (score ">" 1.0) within strict computational budgets where baselines failed.
solution: Introduces the "Plan-Execute-Summarize" (PES) cognitive paradigm, where a Planner strategizes, an Executor implements and locally verifies, and a Summarizer reflects to generate structured insights for a long-term cognitive memory.

Develops a Hybrid Evolutionary Memory System incorporating a multi-island distributed topology, MAP-Elites with feature grids for niche preservation, and adaptive Boltzmann selection for dynamic exploration-exploitation balance.
Frames the evolutionary process as a Markov Decision Process, with the LLM acting as a learnable semantic operator that guides the search and learning based on structured feedback.
paper_summary.summary: Baidu Inc. developed LoongFlow, a self-evolving agent framework that integrates Large Language Models (LLMs) into a "Plan-Execute-Summarize" (PES) cognitive loop and a hybrid evolutionary memory system for directed evolutionary search. This framework achieved state-of-the-art solution quality on algorithmic discovery benchmarks and demonstrated over 60% improvement in evolutionary efficiency across various tasks, notably requiring fewer evaluations to reach target scores.

title: LoongFlow: Directed Evolutionary Search via a Cognitive Plan-Execute-Summarize Paradigm

abstract: Modern compilers rely on hand-crafted heuristics to guide optimization passes. These human-designed rules often struggle to adapt to the complexity of modern software and hardware and lead to high maintenance burden. To address this challenge, we present Magellan, an agentic framework that evolves the compiler pass itself by synthesizing executable C++ decision logic. Magellan couples an LLM coding agent with evolutionary search and autotuning in a closed loop of generation, evaluation on user-provided macro-benchmarks, and refinement, producing compact heuristics that integrate directly into existing compilers. Across several production optimization tasks, Magellan discovers policies that match or surpass expert baselines. In LLVM function inlining, Magellan synthesizes new heuristics that outperform decades of manual engineering for both binary-size reduction and end-to-end performance. In register allocation, it learns a concise priority rule for live-range processing that matches intricate human-designed policies on a large-scale workload. We also report preliminary results on XLA problems, demonstrating portability beyond LLVM with reduced engineering effort.

keyInsights: A hierarchical search strategy combining LLM code generation with black-box autotuning for parameters dramatically enhances search efficiency and reduces invalid code samples.
The approach yields compact, human-readable C++ policies that are directly deployable and maintainable, avoiding the complexities of neural network inference integration.
AI-driven evolutionary program synthesis can uncover superior compiler heuristics, even for optimizations refined over decades by human experts.
originalProblem: Designing and maintaining effective compiler optimization heuristics is increasingly complex and burdensome for human experts, leading to suboptimal performance.
Existing AI approaches for compiler optimization often bypass compilers or focus on per-program sequences, incurring high overhead or compromising correctness.
Significant untapped optimization potential remains even in fundamental, long-studied compiler passes.
results: Achieved up to 5.23% binary size reduction and 0.61% end-to-end performance improvement for LLVM function inlining over expert-crafted heuristics.
Discovered policies were compact (e.g., 143 lines for inlining) and demonstrated strong temporal and domain generalization across various production binaries.
Showcased broader applicability by matching expert performance in LLVM register allocation, improving XLA graph rewriting by 7%, and achieving competitive results for XLA auto-sharding.
solution: Magellan employs an LLM-powered agent to synthesize executable C++ code for compiler optimization policies, directly integrating into existing compiler infrastructures.
The framework utilizes a hierarchical search that decouples high-level policy generation by an LLM from low-level numerical hyperparameter tuning via an external autotuner.
It operates in a closed-loop system, iteratively refining policies through proposal, local evaluation, hyperparameter tuning, and feedback incorporation.
paper_summary.summary: Magellan introduces an autonomous framework utilizing an LLM-powered evolutionary search to discover and synthesize C++ compiler optimization heuristics. This system achieved binary size reductions of up to 5.23% and performance improvements of 0.61% over LLVM's expert-tuned baselines for function inlining, producing compact and deployable code.

title: Magellan: Autonomous Discovery of Novel Compiler Optimization Heuristics with AlphaEvolve

abstract: Optimizing large-scale machine learning systems, such as recommendation models for global video platforms, requires navigating a massive hyperparameter search space and, more critically, designing sophisticated optimizers, architectures, and reward functions to capture nuanced user behaviors. Achieving substantial improvements in these areas is a non-trivial task, traditionally relying on extensive manual iterations to test new hypotheses. We propose a self-evolving system that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs), specifically those from Google's Gemini family, to autonomously generate, train, and deploy high-performing, complex model changes within an end-to-end automated workflow. The self-evolving system is comprised of an Offline Agent (Inner Loop) that performs high-throughput hypothesis generation using proxy metrics, and an Online Agent (Outer Loop) that validates candidates against delayed north star business metrics in live production. Our agents act as specialized Machine Learning Engineers (MLEs): they exhibit deep reasoning capabilities, discovering novel improvements in optimization algorithms and model architecture, and formulating innovative reward functions that target long-term user engagement. The effectiveness of this approach is demonstrated through several successful production launches at YouTube, confirming that autonomous, LLM-driven evolution can surpass traditional engineering workflows in both development velocity and model performance.

keyInsights: LLM agents can perform 'semantic discovery' (e.g., inventing multi-objective reward functions) and 'structural innovation' (e.g., proposing new neural architectures), moving beyond simple parameter tuning.
The autonomous system dramatically increased experimental velocity from Θ(1-10) to Θ(100) experiments per week, enabling the exploration of significantly more hypotheses.
The effectiveness of LLM agents is enhanced by larger model sizes (Gemini 2.5 Pro), specific expert persona framing, and comprehensive, sorted historical context from past experiments.
originalProblem: Optimizing recommendation systems is bottlenecked by the intractability of structural design, as traditional AutoML struggles to invent novel neural network architectures.
Designing reward functions for RL-based recommendation systems faces a semantic gap, requiring deep human understanding to capture nuanced, long-term user satisfaction.
The scalability of human-driven iteration limits the exploration of model improvements, as each experiment demands extensive manual effort from engineers.
results: Achieved observable gains in YouTube-level business metrics (e.g., +0.06%) by autonomously discovering improved optimizers and proposing a new Gating Path architecture.

An agent-synthesized multi-objective reward function, incorporating Active Engagement, User-Channel Relationship, and Video Quality, measurably outperformed human-engineered baselines in live traffic.
Demonstrated an 8x reduction in training latency and improved Inner Loop capacity by optimizing batch sizes and optimizer hyperparameters, without sacrificing model performance.
solution: A hierarchical, dual-loop agentic framework featuring an Offline Agent for high-throughput hypothesis generation and an Online Agent for safety-critical production validation.
The Offline Agent employs specialized reasoning personas (Optimizer, Architecture, Reward) equipped with tools like `compute_loss` and `run_sql_query` to propose structural and semantic changes.
A shared 'Experiment Journal' serves as a persistent knowledge base, recording historical outcomes to enable agents to learn from past trials and inform future discovery.
paper_summary.summary: The self-evolving recommendation system from Google Inc. utilizes LLM agents to autonomously optimize large-scale recommendation models end-to-end, as demonstrated by its deployment on YouTube. This system achieved measurable gains in YouTube-level business metrics by identifying improved optimizers, new architectural components, and more effective multi-objective reward functions, alongside an 8x reduction in training latency.

title: Self-Evolving Recommendation System: End-To-End Autonomous Model Optimization With LLM Agents

abstract: We present Kernel-Smith, a framework for high-performance GPU kernel and operator generation that combines a stable evaluation-driven evolutionary agent with an evolution-oriented post-training recipe. On the agent side, Kernel-Smith maintains a population of executable candidates and iteratively improves them using an archive of top-performing and diverse programs together with structured execution feedback on compilation, correctness, and speedup. To make this search reliable, we build backend-specific evaluation services for Triton on NVIDIA GPUs and Maca on MetaX GPUs. On the training side, we convert long-horizon evolution trajectories into step-centric supervision and reinforcement learning signals by retaining correctness-preserving, high-gain revisions, so that the model is optimized as a strong local improver inside the evolutionary loop rather than as a one-shot generator.
