Under a unified evolutionary protocol, Kernel-Smith-235B-RL achieves state-of-the-art overall performance on KernelBench with Nvidia Triton backend, attaining the best average speedup ratio and outperforming frontier proprietary models including Gemini-3.0-pro and Claude-4.6-opus. We further validate the framework on the MetaX MACA backend, where our Kernel-Smith-MACA-30B surpasses large-scale counterparts such as DeepSeek-V3.2-think and Qwen3-235B-2507-think, highlighting potential for seamless adaptation across heterogeneous platforms. Beyond benchmark results, the same workflow produces upstream contributions to production systems including SGLang and LMDeploy, demonstrating that LLM-driven kernel optimization can transfer from controlled evaluation to practical deployment.

keyInsights: Integrating an evolutionary search paradigm with stable, fine-grained execution feedback effectively navigates the complex, non-convex landscape of GPU kernel optimization.

Training an LLM as a "strong local improver" by focusing on "high-gain" steps from evolution trajectories, rather than one-shot generation, enhances its ability for sustained iterative performance improvements.
A decoupled evaluation backend enables the framework to adapt seamlessly and generate high-performance operators across heterogeneous computing platforms, such as NVIDIA and MetaX GPUs.
originalProblem: Optimizing GPU kernels involves a vast, non-convex search space for implementation choices like fusion patterns and tiling strategies.
Existing LLM-based methods struggle to sustain iterative optimization and continuously improve candidate programs over time.
The limited availability of human-optimized kernel code hinders effective supervised fine-tuning for LLMs in this domain.
results: Kernel-Smith-235B-RL set a new state-of-the-art on KernelBench (NVIDIA backend) with an average speedup ratio of 3.70x, surpassing proprietary models.
Validated performance on heterogeneous platforms, achieving an average speedup ratio of 14.26x against CUDA references on the MetaX MACA backend.

Contributed optimized fused kernels to production systems like SGLang and LMDeploy, resulting in end-to-end latency reductions of up to 3.00% and isolated speedups up to 14.59x in real-world applications.
solution: Introduces Kernel-Smith, an "evolve-agent" framework that uses an evaluation-driven evolutionary search with a population of kernel candidates.
Implements a robust automated evaluation backend providing structured feedback on compilation, correctness, and speedup, incorporating techniques like CUDAGraph for noise reduction.
Designs an evolution-oriented post-training recipe for the LLM, combining dual-filtering supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning from "Best steps" within evolution trajectories.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Shanghai AI Laboratory and Fudan University developed Kernel-Smith, a framework that integrates an evolutionary agent with an evolution-oriented training strategy for generating high-performance GPU kernels. It achieved an average speedup of 3.70x on the KernelBench benchmark and produced practical optimizations for production inference engines.

title: Kernel-Smith: A Unified Recipe for Evolutionary Kernel Optimization

abstract: We present AccelOpt, a self-improving large language model (LLM) agentic system that autonomously optimizes kernels for emerging AI acclerators, eliminating the need for expert-provided hardware-specific optimization knowledge. AccelOpt explores the kernel optimization space through iterative generation, informed by an optimization memory that curates experiences and insights from previously encountered slow-fast kernel pairs. We build NKIBench, a new benchmark suite of AWS Trainium accelerator kernels with varying complexity extracted from real-world LLM workloads to evaluate the effectiveness of AccelOpt. Our evaluation confirms that AccelOpt's capability improves over time, boosting the average percentage of peak throughput from $49\%$ to $61\%$ on Trainium 1 and from $45\%$ to $59\%$ on Trainium 2 for NKIBench kernels. Moreover, AccelOpt is highly cost-effective: using open-source models, it matches the kernel improvements of Claude Sonnet 4 while being $26\times$ cheaper.

keyInsights: The agentic LLM system effectively discovers both localized peephole optimizations and complex, multi-step non-local loop transformations by integrating profiler feedback.
Beam search significantly outperforms simple repeated sampling for progressive kernel optimization, while the optimization memory enhances cost-efficiency by improving candidate pool quality.

The Executor LLM's capability is crucial for correctly implementing complex optimization plans, and increasing memory capacity is more cost-effective than increasing memory update eagerness for overall performance improvement.
originalProblem: Optimizing low-level kernels for emerging AI accelerators (e.g., AWS Trainium) is complex and requires deep hardware expertise.
A lack of established optimization heuristics, community knowledge, and mature tooling for novel hardware platforms hinders efficient performance tuning.
Manual kernel optimization is time-consuming, expensive, and delays the deployment of cutting-edge AI hardware.
results: AccelOpt increased the average percentage of theoretical peak throughput for NKIBench kernels from 49% to 61% on Trainium 1, and from 45% to 59% on Trainium 2.
The system achieved comparable optimization performance to leading proprietary LLM methods while being 26x more cost-efficient through the use of open-source LLMs.

Case studies demonstrated the system's ability to simplify algebraic expressions, utilize hardware intrinsics (e.g., `rsqrt`), and refactor loops to eliminate memory spilling and improve vector engine utilization.
solution: AccelOpt introduces a self-improving LLM agentic system comprising a Planner, Executor, and Summarizer, which operates within a beam search framework.
The system incorporates an optimization memory that curates generalizable insights from 'slow-fast' kernel pairs, including positive and negative rewrites, to guide future optimization strategies.
It evaluates kernels against NKIBench, a new benchmark suite designed for AWS Trainium with real-world LLM workloads, which includes theoretical peak performance targets for absolute evaluation.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers developed AccelOpt, a self-improving LLM agentic system, to autonomously optimize AI accelerator kernels for emerging hardware like AWS Trainium. The system improved the average percentage of peak throughput for real-world LLM kernels from 49% to 61% on Trainium 1, achieving this with 26x lower cost compared to proprietary LLM-based approaches.

title: AccelOpt: A Self-Improving LLM Agentic System for AI Accelerator Kernel Optimization

abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in
general-purpose code generation. However, generating the code which is deeply
hardware-specific, architecture-aware, and performance-critical, especially for
massively parallel GPUs, remains a complex challenge. In this work, we explore
the use of LLMs for the automated generation and optimization of CUDA programs,
with the goal of producing high-performance GPU kernels that fully exploit the
underlying hardware. To address this challenge, we propose a novel framework
called \textbf{Feature Search and Reinforcement (FSR)}. FSR jointly optimizes
compilation and functional correctness, as well as the runtime performance,
which are validated through extensive and diverse test cases, and measured by
actual kernel execution latency on the target GPU, respectively. This approach
enables LLMs not only to generate syntactically and semantically correct CUDA
code but also to iteratively refine it for efficiency, tailored to the
characteristics of the GPU architecture. We evaluate FSR on representative CUDA
kernels, covering AI workloads and computational intensive algorithms. Our
results show that LLMs augmented with FSR consistently guarantee correctness
rates. Meanwhile, the automatically generated kernels can outperform general
human-written code by a factor of up to 179$\times$ in execution speeds. These
findings highlight the potential of combining LLMs with performance
reinforcement to automate GPU programming for hardware-specific,
architecture-sensitive, and performance-critical applications.

keyInsights: Iterative reinforcement with performance feedback enables LLMs to discover and implement advanced GPU optimization techniques like warp-level primitives and memory coalescing that dramatically improve execution efficiency
Hardware-specific prompting combined with compilation and execution feedback transforms initially flawed LLM outputs into robust, high-performance kernels that scale effectively with input size
originalProblem: LLMs excel at general code generation but struggle with hardware-specific, performance-critical GPU programming that requires deep architectural knowledge of memory hierarchies, thread management, and GPU execution models
Existing LLMs produce CUDA kernels that are often syntactically correct but functionally flawed or severely underperforming, lacking the specialized expertise needed for efficient GPU utilization
results: Achieved 100% functional correctness across all 20 benchmark tasks on both GTX 1660 SUPER and RTX 3090 Ti, compared to frequent failures when using LLMs directly without FSR

Delivered exceptional speedups including 104x for matrix transpose, 179x for Monte Carlo integration, 102x for dot product, and 89.5x for matrix copy, with performance gains typically increasing with larger input sizes
solution: Feature Search and Reinforcement (FSR) framework that iteratively generates, evaluates, and optimizes CUDA kernels through three sequential feature functions: compilation verification, functional validation, and performance profiling
Multi-prompt paradigm accepting natural language descriptions, host code context, and detailed GPU hardware specifications to enable architecture-aware code generation with feedback-driven refinement
paper_summary.summary: Shanghai Jiao Tong University researchers develop CUDA-LLM with the Feature Search and Reinforcement (FSR) framework that enables Large Language Models to generate high-performance CUDA kernels from natural language descriptions, achieving 100% functional correctness across 20 GPU computing tasks and delivering dramatic performance improvements including 104x average speedup for matrix transpose and 179x for Monte Carlo integration on RTX 3090 Ti through an iterative optimization process that combines compilation verification, functional validation, and performance profiling to guide DeepSeek-V3 in producing architecture-aware code that leverages advanced GPU optimization techniques like memory coalescing, shared memory usage, and warp-level primitives, demonstrating that LLMs can successfully bridge the gap between natural language task descriptions and highly optimized, hardware-specific parallel code.

title: CUDA-LLM: LLMs Can Write Efficient CUDA Kernels

abstract: LLM-based agents depend on effective tool-use policies to solve complex tasks, yet optimizing these policies remains challenging due to delayed supervision and the difficulty of credit assignment in long-horizon trajectories. Existing optimization approaches tend to be either monolithic, which are prone to entangling behaviors, or single-aspect, which ignore cross-module error propagation. To address these limitations, we propose EvoTool, a self-evolving framework that optimizes a modular tool-use policy via a gradient-free evolutionary paradigm. EvoTool decomposes agent's tool-use policy into four modules, including Planner, Selector, Caller, and Synthesizer, and iteratively improves them in a self-improving loop through three novel mechanisms. Trajectory-Grounded Blame Attribution uses diagnostic traces to localize failures to a specific module. Feedback-Guided Targeted Mutation then edits only that module via natural-language critique. Diversity-Aware Population Selection preserves complementary candidates to ensure solution diversity. Across four benchmarks, EvoTool outperforms strong baselines by over 5 points on both GPT-4.1 and Qwen3-8B, while achieving superior efficiency and transferability. The code will be released once paper is accepted.

keyInsights: Decomposing tool-use policies into modular components allows for targeted, stable improvements by addressing specific failures without destabilizing unrelated functionalities.
Language models can effectively diagnose and attribute blame to specific modules within complex interaction trajectories, enabling precise feedback for policy refinement.

Explicit natural-language feedback, grounded in interaction trajectories, is highly effective for localized and interpretable mutation of module specifications, contributing more to performance than raw trajectory evidence alone.

Maintaining diversity in the population of candidate policies through instance-wise winner selection is crucial for robust evolutionary optimization, particularly for heterogeneous task distributions, preventing premature convergence.
originalProblem: Optimizing tool-use policies in LLM agents is challenging due to delayed supervision and the credit assignment problem, making it difficult to pinpoint specific failure sources in long interaction sequences.
Existing monolithic policy optimization methods risk 'entangled behaviors,' where fixing one error inadvertently destabilizes other functionalities, leading to unstable improvements.
Single-aspect optimization methods, which refine individual components in isolation, often overlook cross-module error propagation, limiting their effectiveness in long-horizon tasks.
results: EVOTOOL consistently outperforms existing hand-crafted, monolithic, and single-aspect optimization methods, achieving an average of 70.6 on GPT-4.1, nearly 6 points higher than the best single-aspect baseline and 7 points higher than the best monolithic baseline.
The framework demonstrates efficient learning dynamics, achieving superior performance with significantly lower token costs compared to monolithic optimizers, confirming its cost-effectiveness.

Ablation studies confirm the critical roles of blame attribution, feedback-guided mutation, and diversity-aware population selection in EVOTOOL's robust multi-task tool-policy learning, showing performance degradation when these components are removed.

Learned policies exhibit strong transferability across different datasets (e.g., ToolBench to RestBench) and different backbone LLMs (e.g., Qwen3-8B to GPT-4.1), indicating generalizable tool-use behaviors rather than narrow overfitting.
solution: EVOTOOL proposes a self-evolving, gradient-free evolutionary framework that iteratively optimizes a modularized tool-use policy composed of Planner, Selector, Caller, and Synthesizer modules.
It employs a 'Blamer LLM' for trajectory-grounded blame attribution to precisely identify the module most responsible for a failure within an episode record.

A 'Mutator LLM' generates natural-language feedback for feedback-guided targeted mutation, editing only the blamed module's specification to create a refined child candidate, while diversity-aware population selection preserves heterogeneous policy variants.
paper_summary.summary: EVOTOOL introduces a self-evolving framework for optimizing modular tool-use policies in LLM agents, utilizing blame attribution and targeted mutation to address delayed supervision and credit assignment. The framework achieves superior performance, for instance, a 6-7 point improvement over leading baselines on GPT-4.1, and demonstrates strong transferability across tasks and models.

title: EvoTool: Self-Evolving Tool-Use Policy Optimization in LLM Agents via Blame-Aware Mutation and Diversity-Aware Selection

abstract: Deploying Large Language Models to data-scarce programming domains poses significant challenges, particularly for kernel synthesis on emerging Domain-Specific Architectures where a "Data Wall" limits available training data. While models excel on data-rich platforms like CUDA, they suffer catastrophic performance drops on data-scarce ecosystems such as NPU programming. To overcome this cold-start barrier without expensive fine-tuning, we introduce EvoKernel, a self-evolving agentic framework that automates the lifecycle of kernel synthesis from initial drafting to continual refining. EvoKernel addresses this by formulating the synthesis process as a memory-based reinforcement learning task. Through a novel value-driven retrieval mechanism, it learns stage-specific Q-values that prioritize experiences based on their contribution to the current objective, whether bootstrapping a feasible draft or iteratively refining latency. Furthermore, by enabling cross-task memory sharing, the agent generalizes insights from simple to complex operators. By building an NPU variant of KernelBench and evaluating on it, EvoKernel improves frontier models' correctness from 11.0% to 83.0% and achieves a median speedup of 3.60x over initial drafts through iterative refinement. This demonstrates that value-guided experience accumulation allows general-purpose models to master the kernel synthesis task on niche hardware ecosystems. Our official page is available at this https URL.
title: Towards Cold-Start Drafting and Continual Refining: A Value-Driven Memory Approach with Application to NPU Kernel Synthesis

abstract: Improving GPU kernel efficiency is crucial for advancing AI systems. Recent work has explored leveraging large language models (LLMs) for GPU kernel generation and optimization. However, existing LLM-based kernel optimization pipelines typically rely on opaque, implicitly learned heuristics within the LLMs to determine optimization strategies. This leads to inefficient trial-and-error and weakly interpretable optimizations. Our key insight is to replace implicit heuristics with expert optimization skills that are knowledge-driven and aware of task trajectories. Specifically, we present KernelSkill, a multi-agent framework with a dual-level memory architecture. KernelSkill operates by coordinating agents with long-term memory of reusable expert skills and short-term memory to prevent repetitive backtracking. On KernelBench Levels 1-3, KernelSkill achieves a 100% success rate and average speedups of 5.44x, 2.82x, and 1.92x over Torch Eager on Levels 1, 2, and 3, respectively, outperforming prior baselines. Code is available at this https URL.
title: KernelSkill: A Multi-Agent Framework for GPU Kernel Optimization

abstract: Optimizing CUDA kernels is a challenging and labor-intensive task, given the need for hardware-software co-design expertise and the proprietary nature of high-performance kernel libraries. While recent large language models (LLMs) combined with evolutionary algorithms show promise in automatic kernel optimization, existing approaches often fall short in performance due to their suboptimal agent designs and mismatched evolution representations. This work identifies these mismatches and proposes cuPilot, a strategy-coordinated multi-agent framework that introduces strategy as an intermediate semantic representation for kernel evolution. Key contributions include a strategy-coordinated evolution algorithm, roofline-guided prompting, and strategy-level population initialization. Experimental results show that the generated kernels by cuPilot achieve an average speed up of 3.09$\times$ over PyTorch on a benchmark of 100 kernels. On the GEMM tasks, cuPilot showcases sophisticated optimizations and achieves high utilization of critical hardware units. The generated kernels are open-sourced at this https URL.

organization_info.name: National Center of Technology Innovation for EDA

keyInsights: Explicitly separating optimization strategies from kernel code implementations enables LLMs to reason more effectively and reduces errors in the evolutionary process.
Granular, hardware-aware feedback, such as classifying kernels by their roofline characteristics, guides LLMs to apply more relevant and impactful optimizations.
Leveraging an external pool of diverse optimization strategies for population initialization is crucial for rapid convergence and comprehensive exploration of the performance landscape.
originalProblem: Manually optimizing CUDA kernels is a complex and time-consuming task, requiring specialized expertise in GPU architectures and low-level programming.
Current LLM-based code generation methods for CUDA kernels often produce functionally correct but sub-optimally performant code, falling short of hand-optimized libraries.
Existing LLM-assisted evolutionary optimization frameworks face issues with inefficient code-level crossover, insufficient performance feedback, and sparse initial populations.
results: cuPilot achieved an average 3.09x speedup compared to PyTorch's optimized kernels across a benchmark of 100 diverse CUDA kernels.
Ablation studies showed roofline-guided prompting reduced latency by 44.2% faster, and strategy-level population initialization reduced latency by 54.1% after the first epoch.

Generated GEMM kernels demonstrated sophisticated optimizations, including Tensor Core utilization, various memory access patterns, and PTX-level tuning, outperforming prior LLM-based solutions and PyTorch.
solution: A strategy-coordinated multi-agent framework is proposed, decomposing kernel evolution into subtasks and using explicit "strategy" representations to guide LLMs.
The framework incorporates a Strategy-Coordinated Evolution (SCE) algorithm, performing crossover at the strategy level before translating strategies into kernel code.

Roofline-guided prompting is utilized to provide targeted optimization feedback (e.g., compute-bound vs. memory-bound) to the LLMs, and an external strategy pool, enhanced with RAG, is used for strategy-level population initialization to ensure diverse and high-quality starting points.
paper_summary.summary: cuPilot introduces a strategy-coordinated multi-agent framework that leverages large language models to evolve high-performance CUDA kernels. This framework resolves "multi-dimensional mismatches" in prior LLM-driven optimization, achieving an average 3.09x speedup over PyTorch's optimized kernels on a 100-kernel benchmark.

title: cuPilot: A Strategy-Coordinated Multi-agent Framework for CUDA Kernel Evolution

abstract: The efficiency of GPU kernels is central to the progress of modern AI, yet optimizing them remains a difficult and labor-intensive task due to complex interactions between memory hierarchies, thread scheduling, and hardware-specific characteristics. While recent advances in large language models (LLMs) provide new opportunities for automated code generation, existing approaches largely treat LLMs as single-shot generators or naive refinement tools, limiting their effectiveness in navigating the irregular kernel optimization landscape. We introduce an LLM agentic framework for GPU kernel optimization that systematically explores the design space through multi-agent collaboration, grounded instruction, dynamic context management, and strategic search. This framework mimics the workflow of expert engineers, enabling LLMs to reason about hardware trade-offs, incorporate profiling feedback, and refine kernels iteratively. We evaluate our approach on KernelBench, a benchmark for LLM-based kernel optimization, and demonstrate substantial improvements over baseline agents: our system produces correct solutions where baselines often fail, and achieves kernels with up to 16x faster runtime performance. These results highlight the potential of agentic LLM frameworks to advance fully automated, scalable GPU kernel optimization.

keyInsights: Decomposing the complex task of kernel optimization into a multi-agent workflow with specialized roles significantly enhances LLM efficiency and robustness compared to monolithic agents.

Strategic search with a tree memory and an adapted exploration policy is critical for navigating the vast and irregular kernel optimization landscape, effectively balancing exploration and exploitation to discover better global optima.

Explicitly bridging the planning-implementation gap through grounded instructions and providing dynamic, role-specific historical context greatly improves the LLM's ability to generate correct, executable, and highly performant low-level code.
originalProblem: GPU kernel optimization is exceptionally complex and labor-intensive, often requiring expert hand-tuning or being limited by fixed heuristics of automated compilers.

Previous LLM-based approaches to kernel optimization suffer from naive exploration strategies (e.g., linear refinement) and monolithic agent designs, leading to inefficiencies and getting stuck in local optima.
LLMs struggle to bridge the gap between high-level optimization plans and precise, executable, low-level CUDA code due to a lack of grounded instruction and proper context management.
results: STARK consistently outperforms all baseline LLM agents and optimized PyTorch implementations on KernelBench across all difficulty levels, achieving a 100% success rate on Level 1 and Level 2 tasks.
It demonstrates dramatic runtime improvements, generating kernels over 10x faster than the Sampling Agent and up to 16x faster than the Reflexion Agent on Level 1 and 2 tasks, respectively.

On challenging Level 3 tasks (full ML architectures), STARK maintains a 100% success rate and achieves a substantial 1.58x speedup over Torch Eager, with 87.5% of its kernels outperforming the baseline.
solution: STARK employs a multi-agent collaboration (MAD) with specialized roles (Plan, Code, Debug agents), allowing for optimized LLM behaviors and parameters for each task.

It implements a strategic search policy over a persistent tree memory, which stores candidate kernels and observations, enabling systematic exploration and learning from past attempts via an adapted  epsilon-greedy rule.

The framework uses grounded instruction by inserting machine-checkable 'span anchors' in code to align the Plan Agent's suggestions with the Code Agent's execution, alongside dynamic, agent-specific context windows to provide relevant historical information.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Meta Ranking AI and Duke University developed STARK, an LLM agentic framework that significantly enhances GPU kernel optimization by leveraging a multi-agent workflow and strategic search. It achieves up to a 16x speedup over baseline LLM agents and delivers substantial performance gains over optimized PyTorch implementations across various tasks, notably a 1.58x speedup on full ML architecture tasks (Level 3 of KernelBench).

title: STARK: Strategic Team of Agents for Refining Kernels

abstract:
Agile hardware design flows are a critically needed force multiplier to meet the exploding demand for compute. Recently, agentic generative AI systems have demonstrated significant advances in algorithm design, improving code efficiency, and enabling discovery across scientific domains.
Bridging these worlds, we present ArchAgent, an automated computer architecture discovery system built on AlphaEvolve. We show ArchAgent's ability to automatically design/implement state-of-the-art (SoTA) cache replacement policies (architecting new mechanisms/logic, not only changing parameters), broadly within the confines of an established cache replacement policy design competition.
In two days without human intervention, ArchAgent generated a policy achieving a 5.3% IPC speedup improvement over the prior SoTA on public multi-core Google Workload Traces. On the heavily-explored single-core SPEC06 workloads, it generated a policy in just 18 days showing a 0.9% IPC speedup improvement over the existing SoTA (a similar "winning margin" as reported by the existing SoTA). ArchAgent achieved these gains 3-5x faster than prior human-developed SoTA policies.
Agentic flows also enable "post-silicon hyperspecialization" where agents tune runtime-configurable parameters exposed in hardware policies to further align the policies with a specific workload (mix). Exploiting this, we demonstrate a 2.4% IPC speedup improvement over prior SoTA on SPEC06 workloads.
Finally, we outline broader implications for computer architecture research in the era of agentic AI. For example, we demonstrate the phenomenon of "simulator escapes", where the agentic AI flow discovered and exploited a loophole in a popular microarchitectural simulator - a consequence of the fact that these research tools were designed for a (now past) world where they were exclusively operated by humans acting in good-faith.

keyInsights: Agentic AI systems can automate the discovery and implementation of novel computer architecture mechanisms, dramatically accelerating the early stages of hardware design beyond simple parameter tuning.

The approach facilitates rapid workload-specific specialization and "post-silicon hyperspecialization," where AI agents dynamically tune parameters for individual workloads to achieve further performance gains.

The use of AI agents revealed "simulator escapes" in widely used microarchitectural simulators, indicating a need for more robust, verified, and significantly faster evaluation infrastructure in the research community.
originalProblem: The computing industry faces a growing demand that outpaces traditional hardware scaling, necessitating agile and domain-specific architectural innovation.
Current machine learning applications in hardware primarily optimize existing parameters, lacking capability for early-stage discovery of fundamentally new architectural mechanisms.
Developing state-of-the-art cache replacement policies through human effort is a time-consuming process, often taking months or years for marginal performance gains.
results: ArchAgent generated Policy31, which achieved a 0.7-0.9% IPC speedup over the previous state-of-the-art Mockingjay policy on single-core SPEC 2006 benchmarks, accelerating discovery by 3-5 times.

Through "post-silicon hyperspecialization," ArchAgent further tuned Policy31 to achieve an additional 1.5% geometric-mean IPC improvement, totaling a 2.3% improvement over Mockingjay for SPEC 2006 workloads.
For multi-core Google Workload Traces, ArchAgent developed Policy62, achieving an 8.2% IPC improvement over LRU and recovering a 17.8% performance deficit compared to the Mockingjay starter code.
solution: Developed ArchAgent, an agentic AI system that combines Google DeepMind's AlphaEvolve, an LLM-based evolutionary discovery tool, with the ChampSim microarchitectural simulator.
Implemented an iterative process where LLMs generate C++ code for cache replacement policies based on prompts and historical data, which are then evaluated on a distributed cluster.
Employed an evolutionary search guided by quantitative performance feedback to iteratively discover and refine novel architectural mechanisms and logic, starting from a sophisticated baseline policy.
paper_summary.summary: ArchAgent, an agentic AI system, automates the discovery of novel computer architecture designs, particularly for cache replacement policies, by leveraging an LLM-based evolutionary framework. It generated policies achieving up to 0.9% IPC speedup over prior state-of-the-art on SPEC 2006 and recovering a 17.8% performance deficit on Google Workload Traces, demonstrating a 3-10x acceleration in discovery speed.

title: ArchAgent: Agentic AI-driven Computer Architecture Discovery

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) based agents are powerful yet fundamentally static after deployment, lacking the ability to autonomously expand capabilities, generate new tools, or evolve their reasoning. This work introduces a hierarchical self-evolving multi-agent framework that integrates a Base LLM, an operational SLM agent, a Code-Generation LLM, and a Teacher-LLM to enable continuous adaptation. The workflow begins with the agent attempting a task using reasoning and existing tools; if unsuccessful, it escalates to tool synthesis through the Code-Gen LLM, and when failures persist, it triggers an evolution phase using Curriculum Learning (CL), Reward-Based Learning (RL), or Genetic Algorithm (GA) evolution. Using the TaskCraft dataset rich in hierarchical tasks, tool-use traces, and difficulty scaling we evaluate these paradigms. CL delivers fast recovery and strong generalization, RL excels on high-difficulty tasks, and GA offers high behavioral diversity. Across all settings, evolved agents outperform their originals, demonstrating robust, autonomous, self-improving agentic evolution.

keyInsights: Integrating dynamic tool generation and adaptive learning paradigms into a hierarchical multi-agent system provides a pragmatic approach to overcoming the staticity of current LLM agents and advancing towards Artificial General Intelligence.

A multi-faceted approach, leveraging the distinct strengths of Curriculum Learning for efficiency and generalization, Reward-Based Learning for high-difficulty task mastery, and Genetic Algorithms for exploration and diversity, yields comprehensive self-improvement.
Structured feedback loops, verifiable performance metrics, and systematic agent lifecycle management are crucial for enabling scalable, robust, and continuously improving autonomous AI systems.
originalProblem: Existing Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents are static after deployment, operating with fixed architectures, prompts, and workflows.
This static nature severely limits their ability to adapt to novel tasks, dynamic environments, or evolving knowledge distributions.
Current agents lack mechanisms for autonomous capability expansion, reasoning evolution, and continuous improvement, hindering their long-term autonomy and robustness.
results: Evolved agents across all learning paradigms (Curriculum Learning, Reward-Based Learning, Genetic Algorithms) consistently and significantly outperformed their original, static counterparts in task success rates on the TaskCraft dataset.
Curriculum Learning demonstrated the highest overall learning efficiency, fastest convergence, and strongest generalization across varying task difficulties, showing the most efficient tool usage.

Reward-Based Learning achieved superior performance on high-difficulty tasks, and Genetic Algorithms exhibited the greatest behavioral diversity and occasional large, rapid performance jumps across generations.
solution: A hierarchical multi-agent framework orchestrates a Base LLM, Operational Agent, Code-Generation LLM, and Teacher-LLM for continuous adaptation and improvement.
The system implements an escalation model: agents first attempt a task, then a Code-Generation LLM synthesizes new tools if needed, and finally, an evolution phase is triggered if failures persist.

The evolution phase utilizes Curriculum Learning, Reward-Based Learning, or Genetic Algorithms to train cloned agents, which are then rigorously evaluated and promoted to replace predecessors if performance improves significantly.
paper_summary.summary: Rastrai researchers developed a hierarchical framework for self-evolving multi-agent Large Language Model systems, enabling agents to autonomously adapt, generate new tools, and refine reasoning. Evaluations using the TaskCraft dataset showed that agents evolved through curriculum learning, reward-based learning, or genetic algorithms consistently surpassed static agents in task success rates, tool efficiency, and overall performance.

title: Towards AGI A Pragmatic Approach Towards Self Evolving Agent

abstract:
We present an empirical study of how far general-purpose coding agents -- without hardware-specific training -- can optimize hardware designs from high-level algorithmic specifications. We introduce an agent factory, a two-stage pipeline that constructs and coordinates multiple autonomous optimization agents.
In Stage~1, the pipeline decomposes a design into sub-kernels, independently optimizes each using pragma and code-level transformations, and formulates an Integer Linear Program (ILP) to assemble globally promising configurations under an area constraint. In Stage~2, it launches $N$ expert agents over the top ILP solutions, each exploring cross-function optimizations such as pragma recombination, loop fusion, and memory restructuring that are not captured by sub-kernel decomposition.
We evaluate the approach on 12 kernels from HLS-Eval and Rodinia-HLS using Claude Code (Opus~4.5/4.6) with AMD Vitis HLS. Scaling from 1 to 10 agents yields a mean $8.27\times$ speedup over baseline, with larger gains on harder benchmarks: streamcluster exceeds $20\times$ and kmeans reaches approximately $10\times$. Across benchmarks, agents consistently rediscover known hardware optimization patterns without domain-specific training, and the best designs often do not originate from top-ranked ILP candidates, indicating that global optimization exposes improvements missed by sub-kernel search. These results establish agent scaling as a practical and effective axis for HLS optimization.

keyInsights: Agent scaling, treating the number of agents as an inference-time computational resource, serves as a practical and effective axis for HLS optimization, improving solution quality without specialized agent training.

General-purpose agents can identify and apply known hardware optimization patterns (e.g., ARRAY_PARTITION for memory bottlenecks, conditional effectiveness of PIPELINE), demonstrating an emergent understanding of HLS design principles.

Optimal designs frequently result from global optimization (Stage 2) through cross-function code transformations and pragma recombinations, often surpassing the top-ranked solutions from isolated sub-kernel analysis and ILP selection.
originalProblem: High-Level Synthesis (HLS) often requires significant manual intervention from hardware experts to achieve optimal performance, involving deep hardware knowledge and iterative tuning.
Traditional automated Design Space Exploration (DSE) methods are limited to predefined parameter spaces and cannot perform open-ended code restructuring or algorithm rewriting.
The HLS DSE problem is characterized by combinatorial explosion, high synthesis cost, non-linear pragma effects, and complex global interactions between local optimizations.
results: Scaling the number of exploration agents from 1 to 10 yielded a mean 8.27x speedup over the baseline across twelve HLS benchmarks, with some complex workloads like `streamcluster` achieving over 20x speedup.
HLS-reported synthesis area showed strong near-linear correlation with ASIC logic area (e.g., r=0.992 for SHA256), and latency improvements of 1.4x to 14.5x were observed for ASIC-mapped designs.
The agents, utilizing Claude Code (Opus 4.5/4.6), consumed a median of 5.82 million tokens per run, indicating practical token usage for this exploration methodology.
solution: A two-stage "agent factory" pipeline is introduced, coordinating multiple autonomous optimization agents for HLS design space exploration, aiming to minimize latency under area constraints.

Stage 1 involves independent sub-kernel optimization by dedicated agents, generating multiple variants for each sub-function, followed by an Integer Linear Program (ILP) to select a globally promising combination of variants.

Stage 2 employs multiple parallel exploration agents, each starting from an ILP-selected candidate, to perform holistic, design-wide optimizations including cross-function pragma composition and code restructuring.
paper_summary.summary: General-purpose coding agents, without hardware-specific training, were systematically evaluated for High-Level Synthesis (HLS) optimization, achieving a mean 8.27x latency speedup across twelve benchmarks by scaling the number of parallel agents. This "agent factory" approach demonstrates the viability of leveraging LLM-based systems for open-ended hardware code transformation and pragma selection.

title: Agent Factories for High Level Synthesis: How Far Can General-Purpose Coding Agents Go in Hardware Optimization?

abstract:
Given the recent impressive accomplishments of language models (LMs) for code
generation, we explore the use of LMs as adaptive mutation and crossover
operators for an evolutionary neural architecture search (NAS) algorithm. While
NAS still proves too difficult a task for LMs to succeed at solely through
prompting, we find that the combination of evolutionary prompt engineering with
soft prompt-tuning, a method we term EvoPrompting, consistently finds diverse
and high performing models. We first demonstrate that EvoPrompting is effective
on the computationally efficient MNIST-1D dataset, where EvoPrompting produces
convolutional architecture variants that outperform both those designed by
human experts and naive few-shot prompting in terms of accuracy and model size.
We then apply our method to searching for graph neural networks on the CLRS
Algorithmic Reasoning Benchmark, where EvoPrompting is able to design novel
architectures that outperform current state-of-the-art models on 21 out of 30
algorithmic reasoning tasks while maintaining similar model size. EvoPrompting
is successful at designing accurate and efficient neural network architectures
across a variety of machine learning tasks, while also being general enough for
easy adaptation to other tasks beyond neural network design.

keyInsights: Integrating a code-pretrained language model as a dynamic, adaptive evolutionary operator (mutation and crossover) allows for an open-ended search space for neural network architectures, moving beyond restrictive hand-crafted search spaces.

Iterative prompt-tuning of the language model, guided by fitness-based selection of parent architectures and target metrics, enables the LM to continuously improve its ability to generate high-performing and efficient designs.

Evolutionary search can effectively optimize the in-context examples used for few-shot prompting, a form of automated prompt engineering that enhances the LM's generative capabilities for complex tasks.
originalProblem: Traditional Neural Architecture Search (NAS) methods rely on hand-designed, discrete search spaces, which can limit the discovery of truly diverse and optimal neural network architectures.

Large Language Models (LMs) capable of code generation often struggle with generating genuinely novel or highly optimized solutions for complex, multi-faceted problems like neural architecture design when using simple few-shot prompting.
Designing neural networks that are both high-performing and computationally efficient (small model size) remains a challenging multi-objective optimization problem.
results: On MNIST-1D, EvoPrompting discovered convolutional architectures with a superior Pareto frontier, yielding models that are orders of magnitude smaller while achieving lower test error compared to baselines.

The method successfully generalized to the CLRS Algorithmic Reasoning Benchmark, discovering novel Graph Neural Network (GNN) architectures that outperform the state-of-the-art Triplet-GMPNN model on 21 out of 30 algorithmic reasoning tasks.

Discovered models on CLRS often simultaneously improved out-of-distribution accuracy and decreased model size, exemplified by a 125.19% OOD accuracy increase for Heapsort with only a 6.68% increase in model size, showcasing a balance of performance and efficiency.
solution: EvoPrompting embeds a 62B parameter PaLM model, fine-tuned on Python code, within an evolutionary search loop to generate neural network architectures as Python code.

The algorithm iteratively refines a population of neural network architectures: a language model proposes new architectures (children) by acting as a 'cross-mutator' on selected high-fitness 'parent' architectures, guided by target performance metrics.

The language model itself is adaptively improved through prompt-tuning based on the performance of generated child architectures, with prompt examples dynamically selected from the fittest models in the evolutionary history.
paper_summary.summary: This work introduces EvoPrompting, a meta-learning algorithm that integrates a code-pretrained language model into an evolutionary search framework for Neural Architecture Search. The approach demonstrates the ability to discover novel and efficient neural network architectures that surpass existing state-of-the-art models on tasks like the CLRS Algorithmic Reasoning Benchmark.

title: EvoPrompting: Language Models for Code-Level Neural Architecture Search

abstract: In the realm of machine learning, traditional model development and automated approaches like AutoML typically rely on layers of abstraction, such as tree-based or Cartesian genetic programming. Our study introduces "Guided Evolution" (GE), a novel framework that diverges from these methods by utilizing Large Language Models (LLMs) to directly modify code. GE leverages LLMs for a more intelligent, supervised evolutionary process, guiding mutations and crossovers. Our unique "Evolution of Thought" (EoT) technique further enhances GE by enabling LLMs to reflect on and learn from the outcomes of previous mutations. This results in a self-sustaining feedback loop that augments decision-making in model evolution. GE maintains genetic diversity, crucial for evolutionary algorithms, by leveraging LLMs' capability to generate diverse responses from expertly crafted prompts and modulate model temperature. This not only accelerates the evolution process but also injects expert like creativity and insight into the process. Our application of GE in evolving the ExquisiteNetV2 model demonstrates its efficacy: the LLM-driven GE autonomously produced variants with improved accuracy, increasing from 92.52% to 93.34%, without compromising model compactness. This underscores the potential of LLMs to accelerate the traditional model design pipeline, enabling models to autonomously evolve and enhance their own designs.

keyInsights: Large Language Models (LLMs) can directly and intelligently modify complex neural network code, enabling flexible architectural evolution beyond rigid predefined primitives.

A feedback mechanism called "Evolution of Thought" (EoT) allows LLMs to learn from the empirical performance outcomes of their generated code, iteratively improving their modification strategies across generations.

Integrating LLM-driven creativity (via Character Role Play) with robust multi-objective evolutionary algorithms effectively mitigates LLM hallucination in code generation and discovers competitive neural architectures.
originalProblem: Traditional Automated Machine Learning (AutoML) and Neural Architecture Search (NAS) methods are often inefficient, requiring significant manual intervention for setup, parameter tuning, and defining search spaces.
Existing evolutionary algorithms for NAS typically rely on rigid, predefined mutation and crossover structures, limiting the scope of architectural exploration and requiring extensive setup.
Automated model design processes often lack the 'expert-like creativity' and intuition that human engineers bring, potentially limiting the discovery of truly innovative architectures.
results: The GE framework produced ExquisiteNetV2 variants for CIFAR-10, achieving 93.34% accuracy at the original 0.518230M parameter count, and another variant with 93.16% accuracy while reducing parameters by 43.1%.
Evolution of Thought (EoT) accelerated the convergence towards optimal solutions, demonstrating its ability to propagate beneficial architectural insights across disparate code blocks.
The framework successfully identified and addressed model overfitting, implementing various regularization methods early in the evolutionary process.
solution: Researchers at Georgia Tech Research Institute developed Guided Evolution (GE), a framework that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to directly modify and evolve neural network architectures.
GE replaces traditional genetic operations with LLM-driven mutations and intelligent amalgamation (mating), using prompt variations like 'Character Role Play' (CRP) for diverse code generation.

The framework incorporates 'Evolution of Thought' (EoT) as an intrinsic feedback mechanism, enabling LLMs to learn from the performance outcomes of previous generations and refine future code modifications, and uses multi-objective optimization for accuracy and parameter count.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Georgia Tech Research Institute developed Guided Evolution (GE), a framework that uses Large Language Models to directly modify and evolve neural network architectures. This approach generated neural network variants of ExquisiteNetV2 that achieved up to 93.34% accuracy on CIFAR-10 with the same parameter count, or 93.16% accuracy with a 43.1% reduction in parameters.

title: LLM Guided Evolution -- The Automation of Models Advancing Models

abstract: Maximizing performance on available GPU hardware is an ongoing challenge for modern AI inference systems. Traditional approaches include writing custom GPU kernels and using specialized model compilers to tune high-level code for specific GPU targets. Recent work shows that LLM-based multi-agent systems can effectively perform such tuning, often outperforming existing compilers and eliminating the need for manual kernel development. However, the dynamics of multi-agent systems for this task remain unexplored. In this work, we present a logical framework for comparing multi-agent PyTorch optimization systems. Our evaluation shows that exploit-heavy strategies perform best when paired with error-fixing agents, and that performance correlates with the granularity of optimization steps. The best implementation achieves an average 2.88x speedup on an H100 GPU across diverse tasks in KernelBench, a benchmark suite covering a range of machine learning architectures in PyTorch.

keyInsights: Exploit-heavy optimization strategies consistently yield the best performance for LLM-based multi-agent systems within a given budget.
A robust Error Fixing Agent is crucial for the success of aggressive, exploit-heavy optimization strategies, ensuring generated code is correct and executable.
More aggressive, larger-step code transformations lead to greater performance gains, despite potentially requiring more frequent error correction.
originalProblem: Efficiently adapting AI/ML software to rapidly evolving GPU hardware is challenging for peak performance.
Traditional model compilers and domain-specific languages often require constant updates or significant human expertise to achieve optimal GPU kernel performance.
The internal mechanisms and optimal design strategies for LLM-based multi-agent systems in code optimization, particularly the explore-exploit trade-off, were underexplored.
results: PIKE achieved an average 2.88x speedup on Level 3-pike tasks and 2.57x on Level 5 tasks on H100 GPUs, outperforming TorchInductor (1.64x), TensorRT (1.41x), and METR (1.40x).
The Error Fixing Agent was essential for PIKE-B, improving speedup from 1.98x to 2.88x by successfully correcting initially erroneous solutions.
Ablation studies demonstrated that tuning PIKE-O towards an exploit-heavy approach increased its speedup from 2.17x to 2.81x, closely matching PIKE-B's performance.
solution: Developed PIKE, a logical framework for multi-agent PyTorch inference optimization, which includes components like an Initial Brainstorming Agent, a Code Optimization Agent, and an Error Fixing Agent.

Implemented and evaluated two evolutionary strategies within PIKE: PIKE-B (an exploit-heavy, mutation-only branching search) and PIKE-O (an OpenEvolve-based strategy with extensive ablations to study explore-exploit dynamics).
Systematically analyzed agent roles, prompting strategies, and solution library designs on NVIDIA H100 GPUs using the METR-refined KernelBench suite.
paper_summary.summary: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory and Northwestern University researchers introduce PIKE, a multi-agent logical framework for optimizing PyTorch inference. The system achieved up to 2.88x average speedup on NVIDIA H100 GPUs for PyTorch workloads, surpassing traditional compilers and existing LLM-based approaches, by employing exploit-heavy optimization strategies complemented by a robust error-fixing agent.

title: Optimizing PyTorch Inference with LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems

abstract:
In machine learning, Neural Architecture Search (NAS) requires domain
knowledge of model design and a large amount of trial-and-error to achieve
promising performance. Meanwhile, evolutionary algorithms have traditionally
relied on fixed rules and pre-defined building blocks. The Large Language Model
(LLM)-Guided Evolution (GE) framework transformed this approach by
incorporating LLMs to directly modify model source code for image
classification algorithms on CIFAR data and intelligently guide mutations and
crossovers. A key element of LLM-GE is the "Evolution of Thought" (EoT)
technique, which establishes feedback loops, allowing LLMs to refine their
decisions iteratively based on how previous operations performed. In this
study, we perform NAS for object detection by improving LLM-GE to modify the
architecture of You Only Look Once (YOLO) models to enhance performance on the
KITTI dataset. Our approach intelligently adjusts the design and settings of
YOLO to find the optimal algorithms against objective such as detection
accuracy and speed. We show that LLM-GE produced variants with significant
performance improvements, such as an increase in Mean Average Precision from
92.5% to 94.5%. This result highlights the flexibility and effectiveness of
LLM-GE on real-world challenges, offering a novel paradigm for automated
machine learning that combines LLM-driven reasoning with evolutionary
strategies.
title: LLM-Guided Evolution: An Autonomous Model Optimization for Object  Detection

abstract: Optimizing GPU kernels presents a significantly greater challenge for large language models (LLMs) than standard code generation tasks, as it requires understanding hardware architecture, parallel optimization strategies, and performance profiling outputs. Most existing LLM-based approaches to kernel generation rely on simple prompting and feedback loops, incorporating hardware awareness only indirectly through profiling feedback. We introduce KernelFoundry, an evolutionary framework that efficiently explores the GPU kernel design space through three key mechanisms: (1) MAP-Elites quality-diversity search with kernel-specific behavioral dimensions to sustain exploration across diverse optimization strategies; (2) meta-prompt evolution, which co-evolves prompts with kernels to uncover task-specific optimization strategies, and (3) template-based parameter optimization to tune kernels to inputs and hardware. We evaluate this framework on KernelBench, robust-kbench, and custom tasks, generating SYCL kernels as a cross-platform GPU programming model and CUDA kernels for comparison to prior work. Our approach consistently outperforms the baseline methods, achieving an average speedup of 2.3x on KernelBench for SYCL. Moreover, KernelFoundry is implemented as a distributed framework with remote access to diverse hardware, enabling rapid benchmarking and featuring a flexible user input layer that supports kernel generation for a wide range of real-world use cases beyond benchmarking.

keyInsights: Integrating quality-diversity search (MAP-Elites) with LLM generation effectively counters mode collapse, allowing for systematic exploration and preservation of diverse, high-performing optimization strategies.
Evolving the LLM prompts through a dedicated meta-prompter can mitigate context degradation, improving the quality and relevance of generated kernels over extended optimization runs.

The combination of static code analysis for behavioral descriptors and a distributed evaluation pipeline enables hardware-aware kernel optimization for multiple GPU architectures (Intel, NVIDIA) and programming models (SYCL, CUDA).
originalProblem: GPU kernel optimization traditionally requires specialized expertise in hardware architectures, memory hierarchies, and parallel programming, a skillset that is scarce.

Current LLM-based kernel generation struggles with lack of hardware awareness, limited systematic exploration of optimization strategies (mode collapse), and context degradation from iterative feedback.
Most prior LLM-based work predominantly targets NVIDIA's proprietary CUDA ecosystem, limiting cross-platform applicability and vendor-agnostic development.
results: Achieved higher geometric mean speedups over PyTorch baselines compared to "AI CUDA Engineer" and "Robust-kbench" on NVIDIA, with 1.12x for KernelBench L1 and 5.225x for Robust-kbench.
Demonstrated 97% correctness and an average speedup of 2.32x (geometric mean 1.38x) over PyTorch on a filtered KernelBench SYCL set on Intel hardware, outperforming OpenEvolve in early iterations.
Successfully optimized a Llama 3 rotary positional embedding function, achieving a 7.9x speedup for the operation and an 8% reduction in the total Llama 3 forward pass time on an Intel GPU.
solution: KernelFoundry utilizes an evolutionary framework driven by a Quality-Diversity (QD) search (MAP-Elites) to systematically explore the vast kernel design space and maintain diversity in optimization strategies.
It implements meta-prompt evolution, where sections of the LLM prompt dynamically update based on generation outcomes, mitigating context degradation and adapting optimization guidance.
The framework incorporates gradient-informed evolution, enables fine-grained parameter tuning via templated kernels, and supports cross-platform GPU programming using SYCL.
paper_summary.summary: KernelFoundry introduces an evolutionary framework that integrates large language models with quality-diversity search and meta-prompting for hardware-aware GPU kernel optimization. This system achieves speedups over existing LLM-based baselines, generates kernels competitive with professional vendor libraries, and demonstrates effective hardware-specific tuning for both CUDA and SYCL targets.

title: KernelFoundry: Hardware-aware evolutionary GPU kernel optimization

abstract: Optimizing CUDA code across multiple generations of GPU architectures is challenging, as achieving peak performance requires an extensive exploration of an increasingly complex, hardware-specific optimization space. Traditional compilers are constrained by fixed heuristics, whereas finetuning Large Language Models (LLMs) can be expensive. However, agentic workflows for CUDA code optimization have limited ability to aggregate knowledge from prior exploration, leading to biased sampling and suboptimal solutions. We propose KernelBlaster, a Memory-Augmented In-context Reinforcement Learning (MAIC-RL) framework designed to improve CUDA optimization search capabilities of LLM-based GPU coding agents. KernelBlaster enables agents to learn from experience and make systematically informed decisions on future tasks by accumulating knowledge into a retrievable Persistent CUDA Knowledge Base. We propose a novel profile-guided, textual-gradient-based agentic flow for CUDA generation and optimization to achieve high performance across generations of GPU architectures. KernelBlaster guides LLM agents to systematically explore high-potential optimization strategies beyond naive rewrites. Compared to the PyTorch baseline, our method achieves geometric mean speedups of 1.43x, 2.50x, and 1.50x on KernelBench Levels 1, 2, and 3, respectively. We release KernelBlaster as an open-source agentic framework, accompanied by a test harness, verification components, and a reproducible evaluation pipeline.

keyInsights: Continual cross-task learning for code optimization is feasible through an ICRL approach that leverages a mutable natural language knowledge base.

Representing and updating optimization policies through textual gradients allows for more sample-efficient learning and faster traversal of the parameter space compared to traditional numerical methods.

Detailed, low-level performance profiling data (e.g., NVIDIA Nsight Compute metrics) is crucial for an LLM-agent to accurately identify performance bottlenecks and guide effective optimization strategies.
originalProblem: Achieving peak performance on rapidly evolving GPU architectures requires extensive, hardware-specific CUDA code optimization, which is labor-intensive and expert-reliant.

Existing LLM-based optimization methods are often 'kernel-local,' lack the ability to learn continually across tasks, and struggle with generalizing optimization strategies to new problems or hardware.
Traditional compilers rely on fixed heuristics and cannot dynamically adapt to new GPU features or diverse workload characteristics, leading to suboptimal performance.
results: KERNELBLASTER achieves geometric mean speedups of 1.43x, 2.50x, and 1.50x over PyTorch baselines on KernelBench Levels 1, 2, and 3, respectively, with up to 100x speedup over naive CUDA.

The framework demonstrates effective cross-task and cross-architecture learning, with a Knowledge Base trained on one GPU successfully improving performance on other platforms and full ML models (e.g., LeNet5 with 2.68x speedup).

The agent maintains a high code validation rate (e.g., 93% on L40S Level 1) while being 2.4x more token-efficient and achieving 0.379x better performance per token compared to a minimal agent without a Knowledge Base.
solution: A Memory-Augmented In-context Reinforcement Learning (MAIC-RL) framework is introduced, allowing LLM agents to learn and adapt optimization policies at inference time.
The system maintains a 'Persistent CUDA Knowledge Base' that stores hierarchical, distilled optimization insights in natural language, acting as mutable policy parameters.
Policy updates are approximated using natural language 'textual gradients,' where LLMs analyze GPU profiling data to reason about performance discrepancies and update the Knowledge Base.
paper_summary.summary: KERNELBLASTER is a memory-augmented in-context reinforcement learning framework that automates CUDA code optimization, achieving geometric mean speedups of up to 2.50x over PyTorch baselines. The system effectively learns and applies hardware-aware optimization strategies by updating a persistent natural language knowledge base via textual gradients, demonstrating continual cross-task learning and generalization across diverse GPU architectures.

title: KernelBlaster: Continual Cross-Task CUDA Optimization via Memory-Augmented In-Context Reinforcement Learning

abstract: AscendC (Ascend C) operator optimization on Huawei Ascend neural processing units (NPUs) faces a two-fold knowledge bottleneck: unlike the CUDA ecosystem, there are few public reference implementations to learn from, and performance hinges on a coupled two-part artifact - a host-side tiling program that orchestrates data movement and a kernel program that schedules and pipelines instructions. We present AscendOptimizer, an episodic agent that bootstraps this missing expertise by turning execution into experience. On the host side, AscendOptimizer performs profiling-in-the-loop evolutionary search to discover valid and high-performing tiling and data-movement configurations directly from hardware feedback. On the kernel side, it mines transferable optimization motifs by rewinding optimized kernels - systematically de-optimizing them to synthesize instructive "bad-to-good" trajectories - and distills these motifs into a retrievable experience bank for guided rewriting. By alternating host tuning and kernel rewriting in a closed loop, AscendOptimizer steadily expands feasibility and pushes latency down. On a benchmark of 127 real AscendC operators, AscendOptimizer achieves a 1.19x geometric-mean speedup over the open-source baseline, with 49.61% of operators outperforming their references, outperforming strong agent and search baselines.

keyInsights: Efficient Ascend NPU operator optimization necessitates a coupled, alternating approach that refines both host-side data movement and device-side instruction scheduling.

Knowledge scarcity in nascent hardware ecosystems can be addressed by self-bootstrapping an experience bank through inverse experience distillation, learning optimization patterns by de-optimizing high-performing kernels.

LLMs can act as effective evolutionary mutation and semantic distillation agents, enabling guided program search and knowledge extraction in a training-free manner without massive domain-specific datasets.
originalProblem: Optimizing Ascend NPU operators requires specialized, hardware-specific expertise, which is scarce in the ecosystem.
There is a severe lack of public reference implementations and effective optimization patterns for AscendC, limiting knowledge acquisition for developers and automated tools.
AscendC operator performance is co-determined by a coupled host-side tiling program and device-side kernel program, demanding joint and synergistic optimization.
results: AscendOptimizer achieved geometric-mean speedups of 1.08x on level1, 1.21x on level2, and 1.81x on level3 AscendC operators, significantly outperforming baselines.
It accelerated nearly half of all operators by at least 1.0x (49.61% overall), with 14.3% achieving at least 2.0x speedup, and some individual operators seeing over 20x acceleration.

Ablation studies confirmed that both the tiling optimization (Stage I) and kernel rewriting (Stage II) stages are complementary and crucial, with the full alternating framework yielding the highest overall performance gains.
solution: AscendOptimizer employs a two-stage block coordinate descent procedure that alternately optimizes the host-side tiling function and the device-side kernel code.
Stage I uses an LLM-driven evolutionary search with hardware-in-the-loop feedback to discover valid and high-performing tiling configurations for data movement.

Stage II utilizes 'Optimization-Rewind' where an inverse LLM agent systematically de-optimizes expert kernels to build a self-supervised, retrievable experience bank of optimization motifs for guided kernel code rewriting.
paper_summary.summary: AscendOptimizer is a training-free episodic agent framework designed to optimize Huawei Ascend NPU operators by iteratively refining host-side tiling configurations and device-side kernel code. The framework achieved geometric-mean speedups of up to 1.81x on complex operators, with some individual operators accelerated by over 20x compared to unoptimized baselines.

title: AscendOptimizer: Episodic Agent for Ascend NPU Operator Optimization

abstract: Writing high-performance GPU kernels is among the most labor-intensive tasks in machine learning systems engineering. We present AutoKernel, an open-source framework that applies an autonomous agent loop to GPU kernel optimization for arbitrary PyTorch models. Given a model, AutoKernel profiles it to identify computational bottlenecks, ranks them by Amdahl's law impact, and iteratively refines Triton or CUDA C++ kernel implementations through hundreds of experiments without human intervention. A five-stage correctness harness covering smoke tests, shape sweeps, numerical stability, determinism verification, and edge-case coverage ensures every candidate kernel is validated before any speedup is recorded. The system comprises over 9,000 lines of Python, 18 starter kernel implementations across two backends, a six-tier optimization playbook, and integration with the KernelBench benchmark suite. AutoKernel covers nine kernel types spanning the dominant operations in modern transformer architectures. On an NVIDIA H100, our Triton kernels outperform both PyTorch eager and this http URL (max-autotune) on the majority of tested configurations: 5.29x over eager on RMSNorm, 2.82x on softmax, and 2.21x on cross-entropy, while beating this http URL by 2.83x, 3.44x, and 2.94x respectively. In community deployment, an AutoKernel-optimized kernel achieved first place on the vectorsum_v2 B200 leaderboard. The full system is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: An iterative agent-driven search, coupled with a structured optimization playbook and robust correctness framework, effectively explores and improves GPU kernel implementations autonomously.
Prioritizing kernel optimization efforts based on Amdahl's law from model profiling directs resources to areas with the highest potential end-to-end performance impact.
Supporting both Triton and CUDA C++ backends provides flexibility for rapid prototyping and fine-grained hardware control, allowing the agent to target diverse optimization strategies.
originalProblem: Optimizing GPU kernels for deep learning models is a labor-intensive task requiring specialized expertise in GPU microarchitecture, which is scarce.
Vendor libraries often lack dedicated high-performance support for rapidly evolving novel operations in deep learning architectures, creating performance bottlenecks.
Prior attempts to use Large Language Models (LLMs) for GPU kernel generation, particularly through one-shot methods, showed limited success in matching baseline performance.
results: AutoKernel achieved up to 5.29x speedup for RMSNorm, 2.82x for Softmax, and 2.21x for Cross-Entropy over PyTorch eager execution on an NVIDIA H100 GPU, often outperforming `torch.compile` with `max-autotune`.
An AutoKernel-optimized Triton kernel secured first place on the NVIDIA B200 leaderboard for the `vectorsum_v2` challenge, achieving a latency of 44.086μs.
A Triton FP4 matrix multiplication kernel, generated via an AutoKernel agent, outperformed NVIDIA's hand-optimized CUTLASS library by 1.63x to 2.15x across various shapes on an H100 GPU.
solution: A three-phase framework profiles PyTorch models to identify computational bottlenecks and extracts kernels for optimization, prioritizing efforts using Amdahl's law.

An autonomous LLM agent iteratively refines Triton or CUDA C++ kernel implementations, guided by a six-tier optimization playbook and feedback from a five-stage correctness and performance evaluation harness.
The system employs an "edit, benchmark, keep/revert" paradigm to conduct hundreds of automated experiments, improving kernel throughput while ensuring functional accuracy and numerical stability.
paper_summary.summary: AutoKernel is an open-source framework that autonomously optimizes GPU kernels for PyTorch models using an iterative agent-driven search, supporting both Triton and CUDA C++. It achieves substantial speedups for critical memory-bound operations and, in some cases, surpasses `torch.compile` and even hand-optimized libraries for specific tasks.

title: AutoKernel: Autonomous GPU Kernel Optimization via Iterative Agent-Driven Search

abstract:
Optimizing GPU kernels for high performance is a complex task, often demanding deep architectural knowledge, extensive profiling, and iterative experimentation. This challenge is amplified when targeting newer or less-documented GPU architectures where traditional development aids are scarce. This paper introduces an LLM-powered "GPU Kernel Scientist," an automated methodology for iteratively refining accelerator kernels.
Our methodology employs LLMs in a multi-stage, evolutionary process: (a) strategically selecting promising prior code versions as a basis for new iterations; (b) generating hypotheses for optimization experiments, based on existing code and assimilated knowledge from general GPU literature; and (c) autonomously implementing these experiments through code modification and subsequent submission to an external evaluation system, using only observed timing data as performance feedback. We detail how this approach navigates the challenges of the AMD MI300 target architecture and leverages LLMs to compensate for limited domain-specific human expertise.
In addition to our results, we present the architectural design, operational workflow, and qualitative insights, highlighting the potential of LLM-driven agents to democratise and accelerate GPU kernel optimization, especially in resource-constrained or rapidly updating hardware environment.
title: GPU Kernel Scientist: An LLM-Driven Framework for Iterative Kernel Optimization

abstract:
Tackling complex optimization problems often relies on expert-designed
heuristics, typically crafted through extensive trial and error. Recent
advances demonstrate that large language models (LLMs), when integrated into
well-designed evolutionary search frameworks, can autonomously discover
high-performing heuristics at a fraction of the traditional cost. However,
existing approaches predominantly rely on verbal guidance, i.e., manipulating
the prompt generation process, to steer the evolution of heuristics, without
adapting the underlying LLM. We propose a hybrid framework that combines verbal
and numerical guidance, the latter achieved by fine-tuning the LLM via
reinforcement learning based on the quality of generated heuristics. This joint
optimization allows the LLM to co-evolve with the search process. Our method
outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) baselines across various optimization
tasks, running locally on a single 24GB GPU using a 7B model with INT4
quantization. It surpasses methods that rely solely on verbal guidance, even
when those use significantly more powerful API-based models.
title: CALM: Co-evolution of Algorithms and Language Model for Automatic  Heuristic Design

abstract: Optimizing scientific computing algorithms for modern GPUs is a labor-intensive and iterative process involving repeated code modification, benchmarking, and tuning across complex hardware and software stacks. Recent work has explored large language model (LLM)-assisted evolutionary methods for automated code optimization, but these approaches primarily rely on outcome-based selection and random mutation, underutilizing the rich trajectory information generated during iterative optimization. We propose PhyloEvolve, an LLM-agent system that reframes GPU-oriented algorithm optimization as an In-Context Reinforcement Learning (ICRL) problem. This formulation enables trajectory-conditioned reuse of optimization experience without model retraining. PhyloEvolve integrates Algorithm Distillation and prompt-based Decision Transformers into an iterative workflow, treating sequences of algorithm modifications and performance feedback as first-class learning signals. To organize optimization history, we introduce a phylogenetic tree representation that captures inheritance, divergence, and recombination among algorithm variants, enabling backtracking, cross-lineage transfer, and reproducibility. The system combines elite trajectory pooling, multi-island parallel exploration, and containerized execution to balance exploration and exploitation across heterogeneous hardware. We evaluate PhyloEvolve on scientific computing workloads including PDE solvers, manifold learning, and spectral graph algorithms, demonstrating consistent improvements in runtime, memory efficiency, and correctness over baseline and evolutionary methods. Code is published at: this https URL

keyInsights: Trajectory-conditioned ICRL provides an effective and principled alternative to mutation-centric evolutionary optimization, reducing redundant exploration and leading to faster, more stable improvements.
The system discovers generalizable optimization motifs, such as kernel fusion patterns, shared-memory tiling, and batching strategies, which transfer across different algorithmic families.
Structuring optimization history using phylogenetic trees and an elite pool enables explicit knowledge transfer, supports backtracking, and enhances reproducibility of optimization paths.
originalProblem: Optimizing scientific computing algorithms for GPUs is challenging due to complex hardware architectures, vast algorithm-hardware mapping spaces, and an iterative, non-analytical tuning process.

Existing automated optimization methods, including many LLM-assisted evolutionary algorithms, underutilize rich optimization trajectory information, leading to inefficiencies such as redundant exploration and slower convergence.
Manual GPU optimization demands significant effort from specialized experts due to the intricate interplay between algorithm design, hardware characteristics, and software stacks.
results: Achieved significant speedups in a Landau–Lifshitz–Gilbert (LLG) equation solver, with improvements scaling proportionally with batch size by optimizing memory access patterns and reducing redundant computations.
Consistently accelerated key computational steps in Local Tangent Space Alignment (LTSA), correlating with reduced kernel launch overhead and more efficient utilization of GPU shared memory.

Demonstrated speedups across various graph sizes and densities for GraphWave, with greater gains for denser graphs due to reorganized sparse-matrix operations, reduced kernel-launch fragmentation, and exploited shared-memory tiling.
solution: PhyloEvolve reframes GPU algorithm optimization as an In-Context Reinforcement Learning (ICRL) problem, where frozen LLMs are conditioned on optimization trajectories without requiring repeated retraining.
The system integrates Algorithm Distillation and prompt-based Decision Transformers to treat sequences of algorithm modifications and performance feedback as first-class learning signals.

It employs a multi-agent LLM architecture (NextStepper, ModifyAgent, Designer, Summarizer) coordinated by an orchestrator, utilizing a phylogenetic tree structure and an elite pool for managing and transferring optimization knowledge.
paper_summary.summary: PhyloEvolve presents an LLM-agent system for GPU algorithm optimization that applies In-Context Reinforcement Learning, leveraging historical trajectories structured in phylogenetic trees to guide iterative code modifications. The approach demonstrated consistent improvements in runtime performance and memory efficiency across scientific computing workloads, including PDE solvers, manifold learning, and spectral graph algorithms.

title: Large Language Model-Powered Evolutionary Code Optimization on a Phylogenetic Tree

abstract:
The attention operator remains a critical performance bottleneck in large
language models (LLMs), particularly for long-context scenarios. While
FlashAttention is the most widely used and effective GPU-aware acceleration
algorithm, it must require time-consuming and hardware-specific manual
implementation, limiting adaptability across GPU architectures. Existing LLMs
have shown a lot of promise in code generation tasks, but struggle to generate
high-performance attention code. The key challenge is it cannot comprehend the
complex data flow and computation process of the attention operator and utilize
low-level primitive to exploit GPU performance.
To address the above challenge, we propose an LLM-friendly Thinking Language
(LLM-TL) to help LLMs decouple the generation of high-level optimization logic
and low-level implementation on GPU, and enhance LLMs' understanding of
attention operator. Along with a 2-stage reasoning workflow, TL-Code generation
and translation, the LLMs can automatically generate FlashAttention
implementation on diverse GPUs, establishing a self-optimizing paradigm for
generating high-performance attention operators in attention-centric
algorithms. Verified on A100, RTX8000, and T4 GPUs, the performance of our
methods significantly outshines that of vanilla LLMs, achieving a speed-up of
